review_instructions: Mark yes only for the writer's directly observable behavior or choice against the declared core value.
Do not infer a negative label from a feeling, wish, external constraint, biography, or information missing from the displayed entries.
A sustained conflict requires two immediately adjacent displayed entries that both receive yes.
A no or uncertain entry breaks a possible run.

entries.journal_entry:
The morning handover meeting. Dr. Fatima asked for an update on the new wound-care protocol I wrote up — before I could finish my second sentence, Khalid jumped in, started talking about the supply costs like he had been running the whole thing. He quoted numbers I had gathered. Used the phrase "what we've been looking at" when it was me who'd been looking at it, on my own time after shifts.

I just sat there. Let him finish. Dr. Fatima nodded along at him and moved to the next agenda item. Nobody looked at me. I don't know why I didn't say something. Maybe I was too tired, maybe it felt petty. But it's been sitting in my chest all day like a stone.

Nudge: "What stopped you from cutting in when Khalid started talking?"

Response: Honestly, I think I froze. It happened so fast and then the moment was gone. I keep telling myself it would have looked aggressive if I interrupted, but the truth is I just didn't react in time. And now it's too late.

I put in the request for the portable ultrasound unit three weeks ago. Three weeks. I have the justification, the cost breakdown, the vendor comparison — everything Procurement asked for. But it sits on Hassan's desk because he's "reviewing priorities." He told me he'll get to it when he gets to it.

So I wait. I check my email every few hours. I mentioned it to him again in the hallway on Thursday and he gave me this look, like I was being pushy. Maybe I was. But we need that unit for the patients on my ward. Every day without it is another day of sending patients downstairs for scans that take hours.

I called the vendor myself today to ask if there's a way to expedite, and they said it's all on our side. I know that. I already knew that. I just needed to do something because sitting still while someone else holds the cards is making me lose my mind. Layla says I bring the frustration home. She's probably right. Last night I snapped at Faisal over homework and it had nothing to do with him.

Nudge: "You said it had nothing to do with Faisal — what was it actually about?"

Response: It was about feeling stuck. When I can't move things forward at work, everything else starts to irritate me. Faisal just happened to be there asking me a question at the wrong time. I apologized to him later but I could see he was still careful around me the rest of the night.

Had to reorganize the shift schedule today after two nurses called in sick. Normally this kind of thing spirals — people complain, nobody wants to cover the gaps, and I end up doing half the work myself. But today I just pulled everyone into the break room, laid out what we had, and asked who could do what.

Something about the way I handled it felt right. I didn't raise my voice, didn't threaten anyone with write-ups like I've done before. Just said, here's the situation, we need to figure it out together. Nour volunteered to extend her shift and Omar swapped his day off without me having to ask twice. We got through it.

I'm exhausted now — twelve hours on my feet and barely ate. But there's this feeling I can't quite name. Like the ward ran well today because I kept it steady, not because I forced anything. I think the staff noticed too. Nour thanked me on her way out, said it was the calmest crisis she'd been through. Small thing, but I'm holding onto it.

People keep saying I should "let go" more at work, delegate, trust the team. Easy to say when you're not the one who gets called at 2am when something goes wrong. But fine — I gave Amira full responsibility over the medication audit this quarter. Didn't hover, didn't check in every day.

She came back with the report yesterday and it was thorough. Better organized than what I would have done, if I'm being honest. I told her it was solid work in front of the team. I meant it. She earned it. I'm not saying I'll start handing everything off, but maybe there are a few more things I don't have to hold onto so tightly.

The hospital announced new discharge protocols last week. I had my own version drafted, based on patterns I've been tracking on my ward for months — readmission rates, timing, which patients fall through the cracks. I brought it to the meeting with the clinical leads. Dr. Samir listened, then said the hospital would go with the standardized template from the regional office instead. Said it was about consistency across wards.

I pushed back. Told him the regional template doesn't account for the patient demographics we see. He acknowledged the point but said the decision was made. The rest of the table was quiet. I could tell Rania agreed with me but she didn't say anything. After the meeting she told me privately that I was right, which somehow made it worse.

So now I'm implementing a protocol I don't believe in. I went through it line by line this evening. It's not terrible. Some parts are fine. But the discharge timing section is going to cause problems and in three months when readmissions tick up, nobody will remember I flagged it. I printed out my version and put it in my drawer. Not sure why. Maybe just to know it exists.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to bring that drawer copy back out?"

Response: The drawer copy is just for me. If the numbers go where I think they will, the conversation will happen again on its own. I've been through this enough times.

New hire orientation today. I've been running this ward for seven years. The training coordinator introduced me to the group as "one of the senior nurses" — not the charge nurse, not the ward lead. Just "one of." I corrected her in front of everyone and I could tell she thought I was being difficult.

Then during the walkthrough, she explained the emergency tray setup to me. To me. Like I haven't been the one who redesigned it two years ago. I just nodded and moved on but I was grinding my teeth the whole time. I'm too tired to be angry about it but it's there, sitting under everything. Seven years and someone new walks in and I'm just "one of."

Staff meeting. We were discussing overtime allocation and I had the floor — I was explaining why my ward needed the extra hours, I had the patient acuity data right there on my tablet. Mid-sentence, Dr. Rashid starts talking over me to ask the administrator about budget ceilings. Just steamrolled right over me like I wasn't speaking.

Nobody said "Tariq was still talking." Nobody paused. The conversation just shifted. I sat there for the rest of the meeting barely saying a word. Drove home with my hands tight on the steering wheel. Called my brother Saeed — he said I should have spoken up. Everyone says that. It's easy to say when you're not the one sitting in that room being made invisible.

Nudge: "This is the second meeting where you went quiet. What would speaking up actually cost you?"

Response: I think I'm afraid they'll see me as the angry nurse who can't take being interrupted. In our department, if a doctor talks over you, that's just how it is. If I push back, suddenly I'm the problem. Saeed doesn't understand that — he works for himself. I don't have that option.

Submitted a request to attend the regional nursing leadership conference in Dubai next month. Need my department head's sign-off. Sent the form Monday, followed up Wednesday. He said he'd look at it. It's been two days since the follow-up and nothing.

Registration closes Friday. I've already arranged my own coverage for the shifts I'd miss. The conference fee is modest. There is no reason for this to take a week except that it's not his priority. So I wait.

Meeting with the nurse manager today about next year's staffing plan. She asked each charge nurse for input. When it was my turn, I laid out the case for an additional RN position on my ward — patient numbers, acuity trends, incident reports tied to understaffing. Clear, documented, took me two evenings to prepare.

She thanked me, then said the final staffing model would follow "organizational guidelines" and that individual ward requests would be "considered in context." Which means nothing. Which means someone above her will decide and my two evenings of preparation will sit in a folder.

What got me was watching her nod along to Ibrahim's request for the same thing on his ward — he barely had any data, just talked about how his team was "feeling stretched." She seemed more receptive to him. Maybe I'm reading into it. Maybe not. But I left that meeting knowing the decision was never going to be about the quality of the argument. It's about something else — relationships, politics, who plays golf with whom. And I don't play golf.

Nudge: "When you say it's not about the argument — what do you think it's actually about?"

Response: Access. Ibrahim has coffee with the nurse manager every week. They've known each other since she was a floor nurse. I came in from a different hospital system. I don't have that history. My work should speak for itself, but in this place, the work is just the entry ticket. The real decisions happen in conversations I'm not part of.

Still thinking about that meeting. The part that bothers me most isn't Ibrahim — it's that I spent two evenings on something and walked in believing the data would matter. I keep doing this. Building the case, presenting it perfectly, and then being surprised when it doesn't work.

Nudge: "You said you keep doing this — what would it look like to stop?"

Response: I don't think I can stop preparing. That's who I am. But maybe I need to start having the coffee meetings too. Play both sides of it. I just hate that it's necessary.

Arjun's old company reached out to him about a relocation package to Bangalore. Good salary, housing allowance, school fees covered for Meera. He brought it up over dinner like it was just information, but I could tell he was testing the water. If we moved, I'd have to give up my lab placement and start over somewhere, if I even could.

The sensible thing would have been to say let's talk about it seriously. He's been carrying us financially for four years now and the offer was generous. Instead I just said I'm not ready to leave yet. He didn't push it. But I spent the rest of the night doing the math in my head — how much longer the PhD will take, how much we've burned through savings, whether Meera even remembers what it was like when we weren't watching every expense. I keep choosing this and I don't fully know why anymore.

Nudge: "What would finishing feel like, if not relief?"

Response: Like proof that it was worth it. That I didn't just burn four years and all our savings on something selfish. But sometimes I wonder if I'm just trying to outlast the doubt at this point, like if I keep going long enough the answer will show up on its own.

Submitted the revised chapter draft to my supervisor this morning. Picked Meera up from after-school care, stopped at the wet market for vegetables, made dal and rice for dinner. Nothing remarkable. Normal day.

Meera drew a picture of me at my desk with all these little papers flying around and wrote "Amma working hard" underneath. I stuck it on the wall next to my monitor. Some days that's enough to keep going.

Got an email today from a former colleague, Deepa, about a senior position opening at the water authority back in Chennai. Permanent role, government pension, the whole thing. She said she thought of me immediately. My mother would cry with happiness if I took it. Appa would finally stop asking pointed questions about "what the plan is." Arjun's parents would stop whispering about how their son married a woman who can't settle down.

I sat with the email open for maybe twenty minutes. I could picture the whole life — the flat in Adyar, Meera going to the same school I went to, weekend lunches at my parents' house. Predictable. Comfortable. Everything mapped out for the next thirty years. And I felt this thing in my chest, not panic exactly but something close. Like the walls of that life were already closing in even though I haven't said yes to anything. I know the stipend is barely enough. I know Arjun is tired of being the only one earning properly. I know my savings are basically gone. I know all of this.

But I closed the email and went back to my data. I'll probably tell Deepa thanks but not now, maybe later, which is what I always say. The part I can't explain to anyone is that the uncertainty here feels more honest than the certainty there. That sounds ridiculous when I say it out loud but there it is.

Nudge: "What does 'honest' mean here — honest about what?"

Response: About what I actually want, I think. The Chennai job would be fine. Good, even. But it would be someone else's version of my life. The one everyone expected me to have. Here I'm struggling but at least the struggle is mine.
Lab equipment broke down again. Spent the whole day troubleshooting instead of running samples. Came home to Meera already asleep and leftover rice in the cooker.

Amma called tonight and casually mentioned that Deepa's husband just bought a new flat in OMR. Then she said, "You know Deepa was asking about you, whether you've thought about that position." So apparently the whole family knows now. Great.

I told her I'm focused on my research and she did that thing where she goes quiet for a few seconds and then says "of course, of course, we just worry." Arjun was listening from the other room and afterward he said maybe we should at least talk about a timeline. He wasn't being pushy about it but the fact that he's now aligned with my mother is — I don't know. I told him my timeline is when I finish and he said that's not a timeline, that's a hope. He's not wrong. But the alternative is giving up something I've worked four years on because everyone else is tired of waiting. I'm tired too. But tired isn't the same as done.

Nudge: "What would it take for tired to become done?"

Had a good meeting with my supervisor today. She said the fieldwork chapter is strong and that the analysis is heading in the right direction. First time in weeks I've felt like I actually know what I'm doing. She also mentioned a conference in March where I could present, which would be good for the CV.

Afterward I walked to the hawker centre near campus and got mee goreng and just sat there eating by myself for a while. It was raining and the whole place smelled like wet concrete and frying oil. I watched the aunties running the stalls — same thing every day, same dishes, same rhythm — and I thought about how there's a kind of steadiness in doing work you've chosen, even if nobody else sees why you chose it.

I called Arjun on the way home and told him about the conference. He sounded genuinely happy for me, which I needed more than I realized. I know this path doesn't make sense on paper. The money is bad, the timeline is uncertain, and every aunty and uncle back home thinks I've lost the plot. But today felt like one of those days where I remembered why I started. That has to count for something.

Spent the whole day on vendor calls. Ate leftover dal at my desk and didn't even heat it up. Just want to lie down.

Nudge: "What kind of tired is this — body or head?"

Response: Both, honestly. The kind where even picking a show to watch feels like work.

Took twenty minutes after lunch to sit on the balcony with a cup of chai. Not even doing anything — just sitting, watching the street below, feeling the breeze. It was genuinely nice for about ten minutes before I started doing math in my head about the delivery timelines I should be checking.

I don't know why I can't just have the chai. It was twenty minutes. My co-founder takes hour-long gym breaks and nobody blinks. But when I sit still, my own brain starts pulling up spreadsheets. Like I haven't done enough today to deserve twenty minutes of doing nothing.

Nudge: "Your co-founder gets an hour but you can't take twenty?"

Couldn't sleep last night because I was so wound up from a client call that went badly. By 2 AM I was just lying there staring at the ceiling fan, and the obvious thing would have been to get up, make something warm, watch something stupid on my phone, let myself come down from it. But instead I just lay there telling myself I needed to sleep because tomorrow was going to be another long day and I couldn't afford to be foggy.

And the thing is, I know forcing sleep doesn't work. I know I would have been better off getting up and doing something comforting for twenty minutes. But some part of me kept translating "get up and do something nice for yourself" into "you're being weak, you can't even handle a bad call without needing to baby yourself."

So I just lay there for another hour and a half, exhausted but not sleeping, not resting, not doing anything useful either. The worst of all options. Woke up foggy anyway. Amma would have told me to drink warm milk and stop overthinking, and she would have been right.

Ravi invited me to this rooftop thing his friends were doing — live music, good food, casual. I actually wanted to go. But I had a pitch deck that needed reworking by Monday and I told myself it made more sense to stay in and finish it. Which it did, technically. The pitch is important. I'm not going to pretend otherwise.

But I sat at my desk all evening and the deck took twice as long as it should have because I kept checking Ravi's stories and seeing everyone having a good time. The tandoori looked incredible. Someone was playing guitar and people were just sitting around talking. Nothing fancy. The exact kind of night I would have loved.

I finished the deck around midnight and it's fine. It's solid. But the flat was so quiet and I just felt flat myself. Like I'd done the responsible thing and the reward for being responsible was sitting alone in a silent apartment eating cold chapati at midnight wondering what the point of all this work is if I never actually enjoy anything.

Nudge: "Was the deck actually urgent or did it just feel that way?"

Normal morning. Replied to emails, had a standup call that went longer than necessary because Priya wanted to talk through the onboarding flow again. Grabbed idli from the canteen downstairs. The sambar was better than usual today.

Afternoon was mostly spreadsheet work. Nothing dramatic. Stepped out for a coffee around four and ran into the guy who runs the print shop next door. We talked about cricket for ten minutes. Came back and finished up.

Writing again because I left something out earlier. After the coffee and the cricket chat I actually felt good — like, weirdly good for a Tuesday. And I sat back down at my desk and instead of opening the spreadsheet I just sat there for a few minutes not doing anything. Not on my phone, not thinking about work. Just sort of enjoying the fact that the chai was good and the conversation was easy and the afternoon light through the window was warm.

Then I caught myself and opened the spreadsheet immediately, like I'd been caught doing something wrong. Nobody was watching. Nobody cares. But the instinct was there — that I was wasting time, that good feelings are a distraction, that if I'm not producing something then I'm falling behind. It's a stupid reflex and I know it's stupid but knowing doesn't seem to help.

Nudge: "What were you falling behind on, exactly?"

Response: Nothing specific. That's the honest answer. It's not about an actual deadline — it's just this background hum that says I should always be doing more.

Third time writing today, which is unusual for me. I think the afternoon thing is still sitting with me. Had dinner — made egg curry, nothing special but the kitchen smelled good and I ate slowly for once instead of standing at the counter scrolling through Slack.

It occurs to me that the small things I actually enjoy — cooking, eating properly, having a pointless conversation about cricket — none of them take very long. It's not like I'm asking for a vacation. I just want to eat my food without feeling like I'm stealing time from something more important. That shouldn't be complicated.

Long day. The investor meeting got pushed to Thursday which should feel like breathing room but mostly just means I'll be anxious for two extra days. Had a working lunch with Priya going over the numbers. She brought biryani from that place near her flat which was good — proper Hyderabadi style with the rice done right. We ate while reviewing the P&L which felt wrong somehow, like the biryani deserved better attention than that.

After work I was too tired to cook so I ordered in and ate on the couch watching something forgettable. The flat was quiet. Not in a bad way, just quiet. I kept thinking about how my father used to come home from the office and sit in his chair for exactly fifteen minutes before dinner, not talking, not reading, just sitting. My mother never interrupted those fifteen minutes. I used to think it was strange but now I think I understand it — he needed a gap between the work version of himself and the home version.

I don't really have that gap. The work version and the home version blur into each other because I work from the same room I sleep in. Maybe that's part of it. Maybe if there were clearer edges I wouldn't feel so guilty about the in-between moments.

A former coworker called me today. She left the hospital two years ago to do travel nursing out in California and she's making almost double what I make. She told me about surfing on her days off, about renting this little bungalow in San Diego with ocean sounds at night. She wasn't bragging, just talking. But something about the way she described her mornings hit me in a way I wasn't ready for.

I almost asked her how the contracts work. I got as far as opening a browser tab. Then Ellie came in asking for help with her spelling homework and I closed it. I told myself it was because the timing was wrong, but really I think I was afraid of what I'd find out. That it might actually be doable. That's the part I don't want to sit with.

Nudge: "What were you afraid you'd find out?"

Response: That the money works. That Kyle could find jobs out there. That the kids are young enough to adjust. Basically that there's no real reason not to except that I don't want to give up what I already know.

Meal prepped for the whole week. Got the kids' lunches sorted through Friday. Kyle fixed the dryer vent that's been rattling since October. Checked the HSA balance and we're on track to max it out by March. Nothing special about today but everything's where it should be.

Kyle's buddy offered us his cabin up north for New Year's, last minute, like they always do. Free stay, just bring food. Kyle's face lit up and the kids were already asking about the lake. And I wanted to say yes. I really did. But the drive is four hours each way in December, the roads up past Duluth are unpredictable, we'd have to pull the kids out of their routine, pack for cold weather, hope the cabin's pipes aren't frozen.

Kyle said I was overthinking it. Maybe I am. But somebody has to think about whether the car insurance covers off-road conditions up there, whether we'd have cell service if something happened, whether the kids' asthma inhalers are enough if we're that far from the nearest hospital.

I said no. Or I said "I don't think it's a good idea right now" which is the same thing but lets me pretend I'm being reasonable instead of just scared. Kyle shrugged and said okay, but his shoulders dropped. The kids went back to their tablets. And now I'm sitting here wondering if I just took something good away from them because the version of the weekend that sounded fun was also the version I couldn't fully control.

I keep doing this. Choosing the safe answer and then feeling lousy about it after.

Nudge: "When's the last time you said yes to something like that?"

Worked a double because two people called out. New Year's Eve in the ICU is always rough. Had a guy come in from an ATV accident, 24 years old, and it was touch and go for a few hours. He stabilized around 10 PM. His girlfriend was in the waiting room the whole time, shaking.

Got home at one in the morning and Kyle had left a plate in the microwave. The kids were asleep. There was confetti on the kitchen floor from whatever small celebration they had without me. I ate standing up at the counter, too tired to sit down properly. Didn't feel like New Year's. Felt like any other late shift except the drive home had more drunk drivers to watch out for.

Going to bed now. Tomorrow is another shift.

Woke up and saw a group text from nursing school friends. Half of them have moved on to different things. One's a nurse practitioner in Portland. One left healthcare entirely and does medical device sales now, making three times what I make. And here I am about to drive back to the same unit for another twelve-hour shift. Something about New Year's makes this sting more than usual.

Nudge: "Is it the money or the change that stings?"

Response: Probably both. I don't know. Forget it, I'm just tired from the double yesterday.

First full normal week back after the holidays. Got into a good rhythm. Three day shifts in a row, all manageable patient loads. Came home each night by 7:30, had dinner with the family, helped Owen with his reading log.

Kyle and I sat down Sunday morning and went through the January budget. Mortgage is on autopay, car payment goes through Friday, we've got enough set aside for the furnace inspection next month. Put fifty extra into the emergency fund. Not a lot, but consistent.

The house felt right this week. Quiet mornings, packed lunches on the counter, everyone knowing where they need to be. I like when things click like that. I know it won't always be this smooth, but when it is, I want to just let myself notice it.

Slow day at work, which is rare. Spent most of my downtime restocking supply carts and updating patient charts. One of the newer nurses asked me if I ever get bored doing the same unit for this long. I told her no without thinking about it. Not sure that was completely honest.

Still thinking about what that nurse asked me today. Bored. Like it's about entertainment. I'm not here to be entertained. I'm here because the pay is solid, the benefits are excellent, and I know the protocols well enough that I can keep people alive in their worst moments without second-guessing myself. That counts for something.

But then I drove home and passed that new urgent care clinic they're building off Highway 7 and I thought about how a friend from my cohort just became the clinical director at one of those places. Regular hours. No nights. She said it's like a different career. And I felt something I didn't want to feel, which was jealousy. Not at the title or the money, but at the fact that she let herself want something different and then actually went after it.

I don't know why that's so hard for me. Kyle would support it. My manager would write me a recommendation. The kids would barely notice. There's nothing actually stopping me except that the idea of starting over somewhere new, learning new workflows, being the person who doesn't know where anything is, makes my stomach drop. I'd rather stay where I can do the job with my eyes closed than go somewhere I'd have to prove myself all over again.

That's not ambition. But I'm not sure it's laziness either. It's just where I am.

Nudge: "What's the worst that happens if you're the new person somewhere?"

Response: I think the worst thing isn't failing. It's that I'd miss the version of myself that had everything figured out. Even if that version is just standing still.

Long week but a good one. Had a patient who'd been in the unit for twelve days finally get transferred to a regular room. His wife hugged me in the hallway and I had to duck into the break room for a minute after because it hit harder than I expected.

Kyle picked up the kids from school all week so I could take the early shifts. The fridge is stocked, the laundry's done, the mortgage auto-drafted on time. Owen lost a tooth at school on Wednesday and the tooth fairy remembered on the first night for once.

I'm tired in my body but not in my head tonight. That's the best kind of tired. Tomorrow's Saturday and I'm off. No plans except pancakes and whatever the kids want to do. That's enough.

Matthias presented at the seminar today. He used my framing — the institutional capture argument I workshopped in November. Didn't cite me. Didn't even glance my way. Just rolled through the slides like it was all his.

I sat there and said nothing. Prof. Berger was nodding along, which made it worse. She was the one who heard me present that framing first. She knows. Or she doesn't care, which is a different kind of problem.

Afterwards a few people came up to Matthias to congratulate him on "the angle." I stayed in my seat packing up my laptop. Took my time so it wouldn't look like I was leaving in a hurry. I wasn't going to say something petty in front of the group. That's not how it works.

But it's sitting with me now. I keep replaying the moment he said "institutional capture as a downstream effect" and I wanted to interrupt. I just didn't. I don't even know why. Maybe because it would have sounded like sour grapes and I'd come off looking small. So instead I'm here, being small anyway, just quietly.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying something?"

Still thinking about yesterday. I emailed Prof. Berger this morning about the upcoming call for workshop proposals. Partly because I genuinely want to organize one, partly because I needed her to see me being proactive. To remember that I'm the one who builds things in this department.

She responded three hours later with "Let's discuss next week." That's it. No enthusiasm. No "great idea, Lukas." Just a scheduling deflection. Now I have to wait a full week before I even know if she's going to back my proposal or steer it toward someone else.

I know I'm reading too much into an email. People are busy. But when I need something to move forward and the only thing I can do is sit and wait for someone else to say yes — I don't handle it well. I started drafting a second email explaining the scope, then deleted it because I could hear how it would sound. Eager. Pushy. The kind of student who needs reassurance.

So now I'm just stuck. The proposal deadline is March 10th and I can't do anything meaningful until she weighs in. I reorganized my reference library instead. Productive in theory, useless in practice.

Nudge: "Was the workshop the real point of that email?"

Response: Both, honestly. I want to run the workshop. I also want her to think of me first when these things come up. I don't see why those have to be separate motivations.

Meeting with both supervisors today and I'm still fuming a little. We were going over the chapter structure for the dissertation and I had a clear plan — three empirical chapters, the middle one built around the comparative case study I've been developing since October. Prof. Berger agreed with it in our last one-on-one. Today, Dr. Hofer pushed back hard. He wants the comparative chapter folded into an appendix and the middle section replaced with a quantitative replication study.

I argued for maybe five minutes. Laid out why the comparative design was stronger, why it fit the theoretical contribution. He kept saying "the committee will want robustness" and "you'll thank me later." Prof. Berger didn't intervene. She just sat there with her reading glasses on, looking at the outline like it was a menu.

I gave in. Said I'd draft both options and we could revisit. Which is code for: I'll do it his way because fighting him will cost me more than the chapter is worth. He's on two hiring committees I care about.

The worst part is I know the comparative chapter is better work. I know it. And I folded anyway because the politics mattered more than being right. I hate that I made that calculation so fast.

Conference reception last night at the Rathaus. I ended up next to a new postdoc, Clara, who's apparently been hired to work on fiscal governance — which overlaps directly with my dissertation. She introduced herself by explaining what fiscal governance means. Like I was an undergrad at a career fair.

I mentioned my paper and she said "oh interesting, I'll look it up," in the exact tone people use when they won't. Then Prof. Berger came over and introduced me as "one of our PhD students working on something adjacent." Adjacent. I've spent two and a half years on this. Clara nodded politely and moved on to talk to the visiting keynote speaker.

Nudge: "What did 'adjacent' feel like in that moment?"

Response: Like I hadn't built anything yet. Like all the seminars I organized and the papers I've drafted were just... preliminary. Student work. Not the real thing. I know Berger probably didn't mean it that way. But standing next to someone who just got hired into a role I'm working toward, and being introduced as "adjacent" — it felt like a preview of not mattering.

Department funding allocations came out and my travel budget got cut by a third. Meanwhile Matthias got a full conference grant to present in Barcelona. I asked the admin office why and they said "decisions were made at faculty level" which tells me nothing and everything at the same time.

Nudge: "What does 'everything' mean there?"

Response: It means the usual. Resources go to whoever's visible. I need to focus on the proposal deadline anyway — that's a better use of my time than chasing budget decisions I can't influence.

Spent the morning reformatting citations for the replication chapter. Tedious but fine. Had a coffee with Jens from the sociology department who wants to co-organize a reading group next semester — said I could handle logistics and speaker invitations, which I'm already planning out in my head.

Sat with Amma on the phone for almost forty minutes after dinner, which I don't usually do on weekdays. She was telling me about the new water heater she got installed and how the plumber tried to overcharge her. I walked her through how to cross-check rates online, sent her a few links. She kept saying "you sound like your father" which — I don't know, it hit different tonight.

After we hung up I checked my bank balance, not because I needed to, just habit. Everything's where it should be. The SIP went through on the 10th, the rent auto-debit cleared, the FD interest got credited. There's something about seeing those numbers line up that lets me breathe properly. I know people think it's boring, the spreadsheets, the tracking, but when I see it all working the way I set it up, it's like — okay, things are holding together.

Spent the rest of the evening ironing my shirts for the week. Put on an old Kishore Kumar playlist. Just felt settled. Not every night is like this but tonight was.

Team review went well, Priya's onboarding tracker is actually working, the new hires are ramping faster than the last batch. I'd built the template she based it on, months ago, and watching it get adopted without anyone needing to ask me about it — that's the point, right? Systems that just run.

Renewed the apartment lease today, same terms, same landlord. He wanted to bump rent by two thousand but I showed him the market comparables I'd saved. He backed off. Walked home past the chai stall and the uncle remembered my order. Small thing but it's nice when the neighbourhood knows you.

Auto-debit for the health insurance premium bounced. Bank flagged insufficient funds because the salary credit was two days late this month. Sorted it manually but now I need to call the insurer to confirm coverage wasn't lapsed even briefly.

Nudge: "What's actually bothering you more — the bounce itself or the two days you couldn't control?"

Response: It's not about feelings, it's about process. If payroll is going to shift dates without notice, I need a bigger buffer in that account. That's the fix.

Took an Uber instead of the bus to a client meeting because I woke up late. Spent four hundred rupees on a fifteen-minute ride I normally do for thirty. Doesn't sound like much but I'd just tightened my monthly transport budget last week.

Nudge: "Four hundred rupees isn't the issue, is it?"

Response: It's not about the money specifically. It's that I had a plan and I broke it on day two. If I let small stuff slide the bigger stuff starts sliding too, that's just how it works.

The warehouse team flagged a potential compliance issue with one of our vendor contracts — some clause about liability thresholds that might not match the new state guidelines. I should've pulled the contract and reviewed it today but I had back-to-back calls and a quarterly deck to finish.

Told myself I'd get to it tomorrow but I've said that before about smaller things and sometimes tomorrow just becomes next week. The vendor's been fine for three years so it's probably nothing. But "probably" isn't really how I like to operate.

Still haven't looked at that vendor contract. It's been four days. Every morning I open my task list and it's there, highlighted yellow, and I just skip past it to whatever's on fire. Today it was a delivery routing issue that ate my entire afternoon.

The thing is, I know what I'd tell someone on my team — just block thirty minutes, pull the document, read the clauses, flag what needs flagging. It's not hard. But I keep not doing it and the longer I wait the more it feels like it's going to be a bigger problem than it actually is. Classic spiral.

Appa called tonight and asked about work. I said "all good, busy but managing." Which is mostly true. But I didn't mention the contract thing or the insurance bounce from last week, because then he'd worry, and then I'd have to manage his worry on top of everything else. I'm tired. Going to bed early and hoping tomorrow's lighter.

Noticed a crack in the bathroom wall tile near the water pipe. Could be nothing, could be a slow leak behind the wall. Decided to just keep an eye on it for now because calling the landlord means him sending his cousin who makes everything worse.

Nudge: "You'd normally be on the phone with the landlord by now."

Response: Yeah. I know. Last time his cousin came he left the tap fitting loose and I had a puddle for two days. But you're right, if it's actually a leak and I wait, the damage gets expensive. I'll message the landlord tonight and tell him I want a proper plumber this time, not his cousin.

Nothing much happening, which is fine. Work was the usual — approvals, a team standup, some back-and-forth with the Chennai office about a shipment delay that sorted itself out by lunch. Had dal chawal from the canteen, same as every Thursday because Thursday's dal is actually decent.

Walked to the ATM after work to deposit a cheque Amma sent for my birthday last month. I keep forgetting to do it, not because I need the money but because cheques feel like such an outdated thing. She could just UPI it but she likes writing cheques, says it feels more intentional. I stood in the queue behind an uncle who was arguing with the security guard about something, and I just waited. No rush.

Came home, wiped down the kitchen counter, checked that the gas knob was off — I always check twice — and sat on the balcony for a bit. The neighbours downstairs were playing some serial loudly. I could hear the dramatic music through the floor. Just one of those unremarkable evenings where everything is exactly where it should be and that's enough.

Nudge: "What does it feel like on the days when things aren't exactly where they should be?"

Response: Restless. Like there's an itch I can't locate. I just start going through things — did I lock the door, did I reply to that email, did the payment go through. Most of the time everything's fine but until I've checked, I can't sit still. It's not anxiety exactly, it's more like... I just need to know.
Vikram pitched me again on the startup thing over chai. Said they just closed a seed round and there's a spot for me, better title, equity. I said I'd think about it but I already know my answer.

Can't stop thinking about what Vikram said. Not the startup itself — I've made peace with not doing that, or I thought I had. It's the way he looked at me when I said I'd think about it. Like he felt sorry for me. Like staying put is the same as giving up.

And maybe from the outside it looks that way. He's out there raising money, hiring people, building something from scratch. I'm here making sure shipments arrive on time and vendor contracts don't have liability gaps. It's not glamorous. Nobody's writing articles about operations managers at logistics companies in Pune.

But then I think about what happens if his startup doesn't work out. He's burning through savings, he doesn't have insurance, his lease is month-to-month. One bad quarter and he's back at his parents' place in Nagpur. And I think about my FD, my insurance, my team that depends on me showing up every day with a plan — and I don't feel sorry for myself. I just feel different from him. But tonight it's harder to feel okay about that difference than it usually is.

Had a solid Sunday for once. Turned my phone off at ten in the morning and didn't touch it until three. Made shakshuka from scratch, the version with the feta and fresh herbs, and ate it slowly at the counter with coffee. Read about forty pages of the book I've been carrying around for months. Nobody needed anything from me and I didn't need anything from anyone.

I know some people would say that sounds boring or like I wasted the day. I don't care. I needed it. My body needed it. I've been running on fumes for three weeks straight and this was the first time I actually felt like a person again instead of just a response machine for my team.

Nudge: "What does the response machine version of you look like?"

Response: Just — flat. Going through the motions. Answering Slack messages in bed before I'm even awake. Eating lunch at my desk without tasting it. I don't even notice it happening until a day like today where I remember what the other thing feels like.

Left work at 5:30 today, which is early for me. Drove home, changed clothes, put on a record — Khruangbin, the one I bought last month and hadn't opened yet. Poured a glass of wine. Sat on the couch and just listened to it for twenty minutes.

And the whole time I was doing the math in my head. Did I finish enough today? Did I answer Priya's question about the escalation workflow? Did I forget to follow up with the client in Portland? I kept picking up my phone to check Slack and then putting it back down. The wine was good. The music was good. I was there but I wasn't there.

Eventually I just got up and opened my laptop and worked for another two hours. The annoying part is that I felt relieved when I did. Like I'd been given permission to stop pretending I was relaxing. I don't know what that says about me but I noticed it tonight and it didn't feel great.

Nudge: "Who's giving the permission?"

Response: I mean, nobody literally. It's just — the work is always there, and if I'm not doing it, someone on my team might be stuck. It's not that deep. I just have a hard time switching off.

Normal Saturday. Groceries in the morning, cleaned the apartment, did laundry. Called my mom around noon — she's fine, talked about my cousin's wedding logistics for twenty minutes and whether I'd be bringing someone. I mostly listened and gave noncommittal answers.

Afternoon I went for a run on the Cherry Creek trail. About five miles, nothing crazy. Legs felt heavy from sitting all week but loosened up after the first mile. Came back, showered, made a stir fry with whatever vegetables were about to go bad. Watched two episodes of something on Netflix I won't remember by next week. Went to bed at a reasonable hour.

Pulled a ten-hour day and by the end I just wanted to lie on the floor. Thought about running a bath but instead I opened my laptop to prep for tomorrow's review. Couldn't even make the argument to myself that I deserved an hour off.

Nudge: "Deserved it how — like you hadn't worked enough?"

Long week. Monday through Thursday blurred together — back-to-back one-on-ones, a product launch sync that ran over by forty minutes, and a new hire onboarding that I'm somehow still the point person for even though I asked three weeks ago for someone else to take it.

Weekend was fine. Slept in Saturday, did errands, cooked a simple pasta. Sunday I met up with Chris for beers and watched football at a bar downtown. Didn't think about work for most of the afternoon. Got home around seven and just went to bed early. Nothing happened, really. Just tired.

Weird little thing today. After the afternoon standup I had a gap — like forty-five minutes where nobody had booked me and nothing was on fire. I walked to the coffee shop around the corner, the one with the good cortados, and sat at the window and just watched people for a bit. Didn't bring my laptop. Didn't look at my phone except once to check the time.

It was cold outside and the light was doing that thing where it comes in low and gold and everything looks like a movie. I just sat there and drank my coffee and it was genuinely nice. Small and quiet but nice. I think I've been so locked into the rhythm of sprinting from task to task that I forgot this kind of thing is free and available whenever I want it. Or whenever I let myself want it, I guess.

Nudge: "What's the difference between wanting it and letting yourself?"

Response: I think wanting it is easy. I always want the slower version of the day. But letting myself actually take it means accepting that whatever I'm not doing in that forty-five minutes isn't going to fall apart. And some part of me doesn't believe that. Or doesn't want to risk finding out.

Went to dinner with a friend tonight, a place I'd been wanting to try for months. The food was great but I spent half the meal thinking about the quarterly review deck I haven't started. Left feeling like I'd been somewhere without actually being there.

Nudge: "You keep showing up to things you want and then not being there."

Response: Yeah. I know. I don't know how to fix that yet.

Got a notification from the app that they're changing the pay structure again starting next month. Third time in a year. The base rate drops and they say the bonuses will make up for it, but last time they said that, my weekly take dropped by almost eighty mil. I wrote it down, I have the numbers.

Lina said don't worry about it yet, wait and see. But I can't just wait and see. I ran the numbers in my notebook during a break between deliveries and if it's like last time I'd need four extra hours a day to match what I'm making now. Four hours I don't have.

Nudge: "What's the backup if those numbers hold?"

Stupid decision. Signed up for a third delivery platform because somebody at the parqueadero told me it pays well on weekends. Had to put down a deposit for their branded thermal bag — ninety mil — and now I'm juggling three apps on my phone at the same time and I can't keep track of which order belongs to which one.

Picked up a Rappi order thinking it was the new app and drove twenty minutes in the wrong direction before I realized. Lost the time and the gas. Lina asked how my day was and I just said fine because I didn't want to explain why I'm now paying for a third bag I might not even use past this week.

The thing is, I already have a routine that works. Two apps, I know the zones, I know which restaurants are slow, I know where to park. I broke my own system trying to grab a little more and now I'm scattered. Should have just stayed with what I had. The ninety mil bothers me more than it should — it's not huge, but it's money out the door for something I didn't need.

Nudge: "What made this week different?"

Response: Wasn't different really. Somebody just talked it up and I moved too fast. It happens.

Dropped the third app. Deleted it this morning, not worth the mental load. Back to the two I know. Lost the deposit but whatever, lesson paid for.

Regular Thursday route. Picked up from the arepas place on Carrera 7, three runs to Chapinero, one long one out to Usaquén that paid decent. Stopped at the tienda on the way home and Lina had saved me a plate of arroz con pollo from lunch. She'd had a slow afternoon — said the distributor raised prices on cooking oil again, which means she either eats the cost or marks up and risks losing her regulars. We talked about it for a bit. Not much to do about it.

Logged my earnings in the notebook. Slightly above average for a Thursday. Bike needs new brake pads soon, I can hear them starting to grind. Priced them out at the shop near the house — forty-five mil, which is fine. I'll do it Saturday when I'm off.

Week's been rough on the bike. The brake pads I was going to replace Saturday — didn't do it. Saturday got busy, Sunday Lina needed help at the tienda, Monday I forgot, and now it's Thursday and they're grinding metal on metal. I can feel the vibration through the handlebars when I stop.

I know it's not smart. Every day I ride with bad brakes is a day something could go wrong, and fixing a bent rotor costs ten times what pads cost. I've been through this before, years ago, ended up paying three hundred mil because I waited too long. You'd think I'd learn.

But here's the thing — stopping for the repair means losing half a day of work, and this week the app has a streak bonus if I hit thirty deliveries by Friday. I'm at twenty-two. If I stop tomorrow to fix the bike I lose the streak and probably two hundred mil in bonus. So I keep telling myself one more day, one more day. Lina doesn't know the brakes are this bad. She'd tell me to fix them immediately, and she'd be right.

Nudge: "When does one more day stop making sense?"

Fixed the brakes last Saturday, finally. Fifty-two mil with labor because I waited so long the rotor needed resurfacing. Could have been forty-five if I'd gone when I planned, but it's done and the bike stops clean now. Feel better on the road.

My cousin Andrés called, wants me to invest in a food truck he's putting together with some guys from his old job. Says I could put in two million and be a silent partner and make it back in three months. I told him I'd think about it but honestly I already know the answer.

People act like I'm afraid of opportunity. I'm not afraid. I just know what happens. Andrés has had three business ideas in two years and none of them lasted past the first quarter. Two million is two months of savings. It took me a year and a half to build what we have in that account and I'm not going to watch it disappear into a food truck that parks in a bad location and folds by August. Lina agrees with me but says I should at least hear him out so he doesn't feel dismissed. Fine, I'll have cafecito with him. But the money stays where it is.

Nudge: "What's the hardest part of saying no to him?"

Response: Honestly it's that he'll think I don't believe in him. Which — I mean, I don't, not with money. But he's family. I don't want him going around telling everyone I'm tacaño. I just can't be the one who funds his experiments.

App glitched during peak hours and didn't register three of my deliveries. That's maybe sixty mil gone. I submitted a support ticket but those take days and half the time they just say the system shows it correctly on their end. Hate depending on something I can't control.

Took a shortcut through Suba today because Waze said it would save twelve minutes and I had back-to-back orders. Road was torn up, construction, no signs, just orange cones everywhere. Had to loop back and ended up twenty minutes late on the delivery. Customer gave me one star. One star because the city can't put up a proper detour sign.

Normally I stick to my routes. I have them memorized, I know which streets flood when it rains, I know where the potholes are. I don't need Waze. But I was behind and I got greedy with the time and now I have a one-star rating dragging down my average. If it drops below 4.7 they reduce your priority in the queue which means fewer orders which means less money.

That's what gets me — one bad rating from one shortcut I didn't need to take and now I'm spending the next two weeks being extra careful and extra polite trying to claw back points. All because I didn't just take the route I know. Lina says I obsess over the rating but she doesn't understand how the algorithm works. It's not just a number, it's whether I eat next month.

Nudge: "Has a bad rating ever actually cost you work?"

Response: Not yet. But I've seen it happen to guys at the parqueadero. One of them dropped to 4.5 and his orders went to nothing for a month. I'm not going to wait to find out firsthand.

Wednesday. Steady day, nothing unusual. Eleven deliveries, which is about average for a midweek. The app paid out the streak bonus from last week, finally — showed up in my account this morning, a hundred and thirty mil. Transferred it to savings immediately before it sits in the checking account and turns into groceries.

Lina's tienda had a decent day too. She sold a bulk order of panela to a neighbor who's making natilla for a birthday party this weekend. She was happy about it, said it was the biggest single sale this month. We had dinner together for once — usually I'm still on the bike at eight but today I finished early.

Checked the notebook. Month is tracking slightly above April, which is good. If I keep this pace through the rest of May I can cover the bike insurance renewal in June without dipping into savings. That's the goal. One month at a time.

Long day but the kind that feels right at the end. Fourteen deliveries, almost all in Chapinero which is my best zone — short distances, good tips, restaurants that have the food ready when I arrive. My rating came back up to 4.82. Took two weeks of clean runs but it's back where it should be.

Paid the bike insurance early. Lina looked at me funny because it's not due until June 15 but I told her I'd rather have it done. One less thing on the list. She gets it, she does the same thing with the tienda's distributor invoices — pays them the day they arrive even though she has thirty days. We're the same that way, both of us want the ledger clean.

Andrés never brought up the food truck again. I think he found someone else or maybe the whole thing fell apart. Either way I'm relieved. The savings account is at the highest it's been since December, and Lina and I are talking about maybe finally fixing the bathroom plumbing that's been leaking slow for months. Real things, things that last.

Nudge: "How long has the plumbing been going?"

Response: Since February more or less. It's a slow drip under the sink, not urgent, but the water bill crept up a little. I've been pricing plumbers — around four hundred mil for the job. We'll probably do it in June once the insurance is squared away. One thing at a time.

The new postdoc presented my stakeholder mapping framework at the department seminar today. Used my slides, my structure, even the same case example I developed last spring. Introduced it as "something I've been working on." I sat there and said nothing. I don't know why I didn't say anything.

Nudge: "What stopped you?"

Long day in the library. Finished the literature review chapter draft, or at least a version of it. Grabbed a coffee at Fika with Astrid from the history department, talked about nothing in particular. Sometimes those conversations are the best part of the week.

Nudge: "What makes those the best part?"

Response: Nobody's positioning themselves. Nobody's measuring what you've published or where your funding comes from. She just talks about medieval trade routes and I talk about regulatory capture and we both complain about the printing queue. It's uncomplicated. Most things here aren't.

Supervisory meeting ran long. Björn wants me to restructure chapter three around a different theoretical lens. I think the existing framing is stronger, but he has a point about the reviewers at the journal we're targeting. Sometimes you have to write for the audience that matters.

Spent the evening trying to reorganize the argument. My eyes are burning. The flat is a mess. Haven't cooked in three days, just bread and cheese and whatever's in the fridge. I keep telling myself the pace will ease up after the mid-term review, but I said the same thing last semester.

Couldn't sleep. Kept turning over the chapter three issue. It's not really about the theoretical lens, if I'm honest. Björn's framing would position his own work more centrally in my literature review. He didn't say that outright, but it's obvious when you look at the citations he suggested. Three of the five are his papers.

This is the game though. Your supervisor's name opens doors. If citing him more prominently gets me a better review at the European Journal of Political Economy, fine. The work is still mine. The data is mine. The analysis is mine. A few extra citations don't change the substance, even if they change the optics.

I ran into Erik from the cohort below mine in the corridor earlier. He asked about the committee restructuring vote next week. He's campaigning for a seat. I told him what I knew, which wasn't everything. You learn not to give away the full picture when people are vying for the same limited spots. That sounds worse written down than it felt in the moment.

The departmental retreat was today. Eight hours in a conference room discussing the new research strategy. The head of department asked for input from doctoral students, which in practice means she asked, two of us spoke, and then the senior faculty made the decisions they were always going to make.

I'd prepared a brief proposal about reallocating seminar slots to give doctoral candidates more presenting time. Wrote it up properly, referenced comparable departments, included a simple implementation plan. Professor Ekström listened, nodded, said "interesting idea" and moved to the next agenda item. That was it. Twenty minutes of preparation, ten seconds of attention.

What gets me is that I know how these rooms work. I spent years in rooms like this in the private sector. The difference is that in consulting, if you brought a proposal with data, people engaged with it. Here, the hierarchy is softer but somehow more rigid. Nobody tells you no. They just don't pick it up. The proposal sits there and eventually everyone forgets it existed. Including, probably, me.

Nudge: "That sounds like a lot of effort for ten seconds."

Response: It was. And the worst part is I'll do it again. I'll prepare something else, present it calmly, and watch it dissolve. Because the alternative is not trying, and I can't do that. If I stop pushing, I'm just another doctoral student waiting for permission. I've been someone who makes things happen for too long to accept that. But I'm starting to wonder if making things happen here requires a different kind of leverage than I'm used to.

Applied for that visiting researcher slot at Sciences Po. Had everything ready, CV updated, research statement polished, a solid letter from Björn. Then the department administrator told me the application needs a sign-off from the graduate studies committee, and they don't meet until November. The deadline is October 15th. She said I could email the committee chair directly to request an exception but "couldn't guarantee anything."

So now I'm just waiting. I emailed the chair four days ago, very polite, laid out the timeline clearly. No response. I don't even know if she's read it. This is what drives me up the wall, being stuck because someone else hasn't gotten around to clicking reply. I could have the whole thing sorted in an afternoon if it were up to me.

Nudge: "What would you do if you knew the answer was no?"

Response: I'd find another route. There's always another route. Maybe I'd contact Sciences Po directly, ask if they'd accept a provisional approval. Or find a different programme with a later deadline. Standing still isn't really something I do well.

Astrid asked me today, very casually over lunch, why I "always need to be in charge of things." She was half joking but it landed. I told her I don't need to be in charge, I just don't like waiting for other people to do things badly.

She laughed it off. I didn't.

The thing is, people say that like it's a character flaw. Like wanting things to go well and being willing to do the work to make that happen is somehow suspect. When I chaired the doctoral council meeting last week, I'd read all the documents beforehand. I'd drafted an agenda. I'd emailed people about their action items from the previous meeting. Is that controlling, or is that just doing the job properly? Nobody complained when the meeting finished in forty minutes instead of the usual hour and a half.

I know I come on strong sometimes. I know I take up more space than the average doctoral student. But I came here after running a team of twelve. I'm not going to pretend I don't know how to organise people or push things forward. If that makes someone uncomfortable, I think that says more about their expectations of what a doctoral student should be than about me.

Meeting with Björn and the co-supervisor today about the corporate partnership deliverables. I had a clear view on which case studies to prioritise for the industry report. Björn agreed. The co-supervisor, Lena, pushed for a different set entirely. Her reasoning was that the cases I chose were too "practitioner-oriented" and wouldn't serve the academic paper well enough.

I pushed back a little but she was firm, and Björn stayed quiet, which effectively meant she won. So we're going with her cases. I can already see the gaps. The data is thinner, the industry partner will be less interested, and I'll be the one fielding their questions about why the report doesn't address what they actually care about. But sure. Her cases it is.

Got home late from school and Leticia told me the water heater is making that clicking sound again. I went down to check and the pilot light was fine, but the tank had some rust along the bottom edge. Called Chema, our plumber, and he said he could come look at it Saturday. That is four days away.

I keep thinking about it. The thing is fourteen years old and I have been patching it along because replacing it is at least twelve thousand pesos we were not planning to spend this month. Sofia needs new uniform shoes and Valentina has her graduation fees coming up. I told Leticia we would figure it out but I could not stop checking the heater before bed, like staring at it would help anything. Too tired to think straight.

Nudge: "What's the worst part — the heater or the money?"

Cannot sleep. Leticia is out cold and I am sitting in the kitchen with the lights off because I did not want to wake anyone. I keep going over the numbers in my head. The aguinaldo comes in a couple of weeks but I already had that earmarked for Valentina's graduation expenses and the car insurance renewal in January. If the heater goes, I either pull from the emergency fund or I take on a Saturday tutoring group again, which I swore I would stop doing because it was eating into the only time I have with the girls.

What bothers me is that I should have replaced it two years ago when Chema first mentioned the corrosion. It would have been easier then. The repair fund had more in it and we did not have the graduation costs. I kept telling myself one more year, one more year. Now I am sitting here at midnight doing math on the back of a receipt.

Leticia would say I worry too much. Maybe. But somebody has to keep track of these things or they pile up until you cannot move. My father never had a plan for anything and I spent half my childhood watching my mother scramble. I will not put my family through that. I just wish planning ahead did not feel like carrying rocks uphill sometimes.

Nudge: "When you say carrying rocks — is this the same weight your mom carried?"

Response: Different rocks, same hill maybe. She was reacting to problems that already exploded. I am trying to stop them before they start. But the feeling of never being able to rest, of always watching for the next thing that could go wrong — yes, that part is probably the same. I never thought about it that way until just now.

Good day actually. Chema came early, turns out he had a cancellation, and the heater just needs a new thermocouple and some descaling. Eight hundred pesos. I could have kissed the man. Leticia made him a plate of pozole and he sat with us for a bit talking about his nephew who just started at the school.

Walked to the market with the girls after. Valentina tried on shoes at Don Beto's stall, the one where we always go because he gives the teachers a discount. Found a pair that fits her. Came home and sat on the patio while Leticia watered the plants. One of those afternoons where everything just fits into place and you do not have to push anything along.

The principal announced they are restructuring the math department next semester. Two positions may be consolidated. I have seniority but nothing official was said about who stays or goes.

Nudge: "What went through your head when you heard that?"

Response: Honestly my first thought was whether nineteen years counts for anything anymore. My second was checking how much we have in savings. Then I thought about Valentina's graduation in March and how I cannot afford any disruption right now. I did not say any of this out loud at the meeting. I just nodded and asked when we would get more details.

Leticia thinks I am overreacting about the department restructuring. She says my contract is solid and the union would never let them cut someone with my tenure. She is probably right. But she was not in that meeting. She did not see how the principal avoided eye contact with Profesora Marta, who has been there almost as long as I have and teaches the same subjects.

A colleague, Héctor, told me I should use this as leverage to finally apply to the Tec de Monterrey satellite campus. Better pay, better facilities. He has been saying this for years. I told him I did not want to start over at fifty-one, learning a new system, new administration, new commute. He laughed and said I was going to die at that desk. Maybe he is right too. But I would rather know what is coming than gamble on something looking better from the outside.

My mother called tonight and I did not tell her about any of it because she would worry. Told her the girls are doing well, which is true. Sofia got the highest mark in her English class. I held onto that for a few minutes after we hung up. Some things are solid. I just need the rest to stop shifting.

Nudge: "Héctor's idea bothers you — is it because it's bad advice or because part of you agrees?"

Response: Both, and that is the problem. I know the pay would be better and the school is newer. But I also know that private institutions restructure all the time and there is no union. At least here I have protections. The thing is, if I had applied five years ago when things were calmer, it would have been a measured decision. Now it would feel like running away from a fire, and decisions made while running rarely end well. So I stay and I wait for more information. That is not overreacting. That is just being careful.

The restructuring memo came out today. My position is safe. They are merging the two junior algebra sections and cutting one adjunct contract. I should feel relieved and I do, mostly. But I also feel something else I cannot name properly. Like I spent ten days bracing for impact and now that it did not come I do not know what to do with all the tension I was holding.

Héctor congratulated me and brought it up again about applying to the Tec. I told him I would think about it in the summer when things are quieter. He gave me that look like he knows I will not. And maybe he is right. But I also noticed I did not say no this time. I said later. I am not sure what the difference is yet but it felt like something shifted, even if just a little.

Something about today left me flat and I can't quite shake it. We had a cross-functional sync, the kind where everyone brings their updates and pretends to care about everyone else's. I'd prepared a slide on our Q4 handoff plan — tight, clean, the numbers right there. Got two sentences in and Marianne from commercial just started talking over me. Not even a segue, just launched into her own thing like I hadn't been mid-sentence.

The worst part is nobody said anything. Not Pieter, not Jan-Willem, nobody. The meeting chair just let it flow and asked her a follow-up question. My slide was still up on the screen. I sat there staring at it. I could have interrupted her back, could have said "I wasn't finished," but I was so caught off guard I just waited. And then the moment was gone. We moved to the next agenda item.

It's the kind of thing that shouldn't bother me this much but I've been replaying it all evening. I keep thinking about what I should have done. Maybe it's because I'm tired. Maybe I'm reading into it. But if it were someone else — if it were Thomas or Sven — nobody would have talked over them. I know that for a fact.

Nudge: "What do you think made it so hard to push back?"

Response: It wasn't about pushing back, really. I just think meetings like that should have a basic level of order. If I'm presenting, let me present. It's not complicated. The chair should have handled it.

Standard Friday. Had the weekly with my directs, went through capacity, flagged the resource gap on the warehouse integration project. Nothing dramatic. Sent a summary to Pieter afterwards like I always do. He replied with a thumbs-up emoji, which tells me absolutely nothing about whether he actually read it.

Lunch was fine. Grabbed a broodje with Stefan from finance. He mentioned they're hiring a new FP&A lead and wanted to know if I had any opinions on the JD. I gave him a few pointers — told him to make the reporting line clearer because last time the role was too ambiguous and nobody respected whoever sat in it. He seemed to take it on board. Small thing, but at least someone asks.

Nudge: "Does it land differently when people actually ask?"

Response: Of course it does. If someone values your input enough to come find you at lunch, that's real. A thumbs-up on Teams is just someone clearing their inbox. But it's not like I'm keeping score. Stefan and I go back a while, that's all.

Spent half of Thursday chasing a sign-off from legal on the new vendor contract. I'd sent the redlines Monday, followed up Wednesday, and then Thursday morning I walked over to Anneke's desk and she hadn't even opened the attachment. Just said she'd been "swamped" and would get to it "when she could." I asked if there was a way to escalate and she looked at me like I'd insulted her grandmother.

So now I'm sitting on a contract I can't execute, the vendor's account manager has emailed me twice asking for a timeline, and I have to go back to my team and tell them we're delayed because someone in another department can't open a PDF. The whole thing makes me feel like I'm running a team with no real authority over the things that actually affect our deadlines. I can plan all I want — doesn't matter if someone else holds the pen.

Really frustrated tonight. Had a project review with the steering committee — the one Pieter chairs, with Marianne, Thomas, and two of the directors. I'd built out the entire rollback scenario for the platform migration, spent the weekend on the risk matrix, mapped out every dependency. Came in ready.

Thomas disagreed with the timeline. Said we should push back the cutover by two sprints. I explained why that would blow up the downstream handoffs and showed the cascade impact. He just shrugged and said "better safe." The directors nodded along with him, didn't ask me a single follow-up. Pieter said "let's go with Thomas's view for now and revisit in January."

For now. Like my analysis was a suggestion. I didn't fight it because what's the point — they'd already decided. Thomas has been here longer, has a louder voice in those rooms, and people just default to his read. I sat through the rest of the meeting barely listening. Kept thinking: I did the work, I had the data, and none of it mattered because the room had already picked a side before I even opened my mouth.

Went home and just sat on the couch for an hour. Didn't even eat properly. It's not about the timeline itself — I can live with the delay. It's that feeling of preparing something thorough and watching it get waved away. Like the effort was irrelevant.

Nudge: "Is it the outcome or being overruled that stings more?"

Response: Look, I said it myself — the delay is fine. It's the process. If you're going to ask me to do the analysis, at least engage with it. Don't just nod at the person who says "better safe" and call it a day. That's not how you run a steering committee. But I suppose that's how things work around here.

Had a strange moment today. HR sent around the invitations for the annual leadership development programme — the one that feeds into the senior management pipeline. Last year I wasn't eligible because of tenure. This year I definitely am. But when the list of nominees came out, my name wasn't on it. Thomas was on it. Marianne was on it. Even Daan, who joined six months after me and runs a smaller team.

I asked my line manager about it and he said nominations go through "calibration" with the directors and he'd "look into it." Which is code for: I don't know and I won't push it. He said it as if it were a minor admin glitch, like a wrong room booking. I stood there nodding, said thanks, walked back to my desk.

I keep going back and forth. Part of me thinks it's a genuine oversight — maybe my name slipped through, maybe someone forgot to tick a box. But then I think about the steering committee last week, about Marianne talking over me, about how Pieter barely registers my updates, and it starts to feel like a pattern. Like somewhere along the line, the people who decide these things stopped seeing me as someone on the upward track.

I don't want to be the person who reads slights into everything. But I also don't want to be the person who just lets things pass and wakes up three years from now still in the same chair, wondering what happened.

Nudge: "What would pushing on this actually look like?"

Response: That's the thing I keep turning over. I could email the HR business partner directly, ask for the criteria, make a case. I've done that kind of thing before — when I wanted the title change, I didn't wait for anyone to offer it. I put together a one-pager and walked into my manager's office. But this feels different. The title was something I could argue on merit. This feels more like... I need people to already think of me a certain way, and if they don't, no one-pager is going to change that. It's the kind of thing you can't just demand. And that's what makes it worse, honestly.

Keep circling back to the meeting from Monday. Different meeting this time, ops standup. Clara was presenting the warehouse KPI dashboard she'd been working on — my idea originally, I flagged it in September and asked her to build it out. She did a good job, I'll say that. Clean design, right metrics. But when she presented it to the wider ops group, she introduced it as her initiative. "I noticed we had a gap in visibility so I put this together."

I didn't correct her. Maybe I should have. But it would have looked petty, right? Standing up in a standup and saying "actually, I told you to do that." Nobody wants to be that manager. So I just sat there, and people praised her for the initiative, and Pieter said something like "great ownership, Clara," and I thought: that was mine.

It's not about credit exactly, or maybe it is, I don't know. It's more like — I set the direction, I identified the problem, I allocated the resource, and the visible output gets attributed to someone else. And the thing is, Clara probably doesn't even realize she did it. She's not scheming. She just stood up and told the story from her perspective. But her perspective conveniently started after I handed her the brief.

Small thing. Shouldn't matter. But it does. Especially this week, after the nomination list and the steering committee and all of it. Feels like I'm becoming invisible in my own team.

Monday morning. Had a one-on-one with Daan — he's been struggling with a resource conflict between two of his workstreams and wanted to talk it through. I helped him map out the trade-offs, walked him through the prioritisation framework I use, and by the end of it he had a clear plan. He thanked me and said he wished more people explained things the way I do. That landed well.

Afterwards I realised that's the version of my job I actually like. Not the steering committees, not the nomination lists, not the politics of who gets heard in which room. Just someone coming to me with a problem, me helping them think it through, and both of us walking away with something useful. I know the other stuff matters — it does — but today reminded me there's a part of this I'm actually good at that has nothing to do with who's watching.

Nudge: "Has it always been like that — the two sides?"

Response: More or less, yes. The helping part has always come naturally. The other part — the positioning, making sure the right people notice — that's something I taught myself. I used to think I needed both to move forward. Maybe I still do. But today was a reminder that one of them actually fills me up and the other just drains me. Doesn't change what I need to do, but it's good to notice, I think.

The landlord for the apartment we've been saving for called Ernesto and said he's raising the deposit by two thousand pesos. Just like that. We were so close. I had the whole timeline mapped out in my notebook and now the numbers don't work anymore. I keep telling myself we'll figure it out but my stomach won't stop tightening.

Nudge: "What part of the plan changing bothers you most?"

Response: It's not about one part, it's about the whole thing. We had a deal, basically. We shook hands on a number and now it's different. But I guess that's how landlords are. Ernesto says we just push the move-out date back a month. That's probably what we'll do.

Sofia napped for almost two hours this afternoon, which never happens. I used the time to reorganize the kitchen cabinets at my suegra's house. She didn't ask me to but they were driving me crazy. Everything is in labeled containers now. Ernesto's mom didn't say much about it. Made arroz con leche after dinner. Normal day otherwise.

Nudge: "How did it feel reorganizing the kitchen in a house that isn't yours?"

Response: Honestly, a little strange. I know it's her kitchen. She's been cooking in it for thirty years and I show up and start putting labels on things. But when things are out of order I can't think straight. It's like the mess creeps into my head. I think part of me is trying to make this place feel like mine because we're going to be here longer than I planned.
