Putting the lentils in a jar with a label is the one thing I can actually control right now.

Ernesto brought up the motorcycle idea again at breakfast. His coworker is selling one cheap because he's moving to Monterrey. It would save Ernesto bus fare, which is like eight hundred pesos a month, and it would cut his commute in half. He showed me the numbers and on paper it makes sense. I get that.

But we'd have to pull from the apartment savings to buy it. Not all of it, but enough that the timeline changes again. And motorcycles break down. Insurance costs money. What if he gets into an accident? His factory doesn't have great coverage for that kind of thing. I didn't say all of this out loud because I could tell he was excited and I didn't want to be the person who always says no.

I said I'd think about it. Which is what I say when I already know my answer but don't want to fight. My mom used to do the same thing with my dad. She'd say "vamos a ver" and then quietly make sure things stayed the way they were.

I looked at our savings notebook after he left for work. If we buy the motorcycle we'd need four more months to save back up for the deposit. Four months is a long time when you're sleeping in someone else's house and your daughter doesn't have her own room.

Made Sofia's nine-month checkup appointment for next week. Packed her bag the night before like I always do—extra clothes, snacks, her vaccination booklet in the zippered pocket. Ernesto laughed and said I prepare like we're going on a trip. Maybe. But last time we forgot the booklet and had to come back. That's not happening again. Felt good having it all ready.

Nudge: "What goes through your mind when everything's packed and ready?"

Response: It's like I can breathe for a second. When the bag is packed and the appointment is confirmed and I know exactly what time we need to leave, there's this little window where nothing can go wrong. Or at least, nothing that I could have prevented. That matters to me. My mom raised four of us and she was always scrambling, always forgetting things, always stressed. I don't want that for Sofia. I want her to feel like someone has it handled.

The water heater has been making a weird sound for about a week. My suegra says it's always done that but I know it hasn't because I would have noticed. I told Ernesto we should call someone to look at it. He said it's probably fine and that getting a repair person out on a weekend costs double.

So we're just going to wait. And I'm the one who bathes Sofia in that bathroom every evening, listening to the thing rattle, wondering if it's going to give out or worse. He's at work all day, he doesn't hear it.

I know I could call someone myself but it's not my house. It's his parents' house and their water heater. So I wait for Ernesto to decide it matters enough, and in the meantime I boil water on the stove just in case, which his mom thinks is ridiculous.

Nudge: "You mentioned boiling water just in case — what would need to happen for you to call someone yourself?"

Sofia is teething again and didn't sleep more than two hours at a stretch last night. I lost count of how many times I got up. Ernesto tried to help but she only wanted me, crying harder when he picked her up, so he went back to bed because he had an early shift.

My suegra made us chilaquiles this morning and I almost fell asleep at the table. She took Sofia for a walk around the block so I could rest. I lay down but couldn't actually sleep. Just stared at the ceiling thinking about nothing specific.

Did laundry, folded everything, put it away. Made a grocery list for the week. The routine helps on days like this. When I'm too tired to think, my hands know what to do. Ernesto texted asking if I needed anything from the tienda on his way home. I said no. We have everything. I checked twice.

Locked in the Trane contract for the Edina office park this morning. Six units, maintenance included, eighteen-month term. The margins are thin but it's recurring revenue and that's what matters right now. Sat in the truck after the handshake and just felt the weight come off for a second. Months of quoting and following up and it's done.

Nudge: "What was the weight about?"

Response: Payroll mostly. We added a third tech in February and every month since then I've been watching the numbers tighter than usual. This contract covers his salary through next winter. That's the weight—knowing I can keep the lights on for the guys who depend on me.

Got home from the Edina signing and Karen had dinner going already. Pork chops, which she only makes when she's in a good mood, so that was nice. Told her about the contract and she said congratulations but then asked if I'd thought more about the Comfort Systems recruiter who left a voicemail last week. Regional operations director, salary plus bonus, full benefits. She'd pulled up the Glassdoor page and had it open on the iPad.

I said I'd look at it later. She said I always say that. Which is true. The recruiter calls every eight or nine months and I never call back. Karen thinks it's stubbornness. Maybe it is. But I built this business from one van and a credit card and walking away from it for a salary feels like admitting something I'm not ready to admit.

We didn't argue about it. We never really argue about it. She makes her case, I deflect, we move on to dishes. The pork chops were good. I checked the bank balance on my phone while she loaded the dishwasher, which is a habit I should probably stop doing at the dinner table. Numbers looked fine. They usually do when I check them four times a day.

Had the best day I've had in months and I need to put it somewhere. One of our installs from last year—the dental clinic on France Ave—called because their system was cycling weird. I drove out myself instead of sending Tyler because I remembered the ductwork in that building is unusual, old construction with some modifications from the seventies.

Spent two hours up in the ceiling tracing the problem. Found it. A damper actuator that was wired backwards from the factory, not our install, original equipment. Fixed it with a twenty-dollar part and the office manager looked at me like I'd performed surgery. She said the last company they used would have quoted them a full replacement.

That right there. That's why I do this. Not the money, not the freedom, but the actual work. Knowing the systems, knowing the buildings, solving the puzzle that nobody else caught because nobody else spent two hours in a ceiling when they could've just sold a new unit. I drove home with the windows down even though it was only fifty-five degrees. Karen asked why I was smiling and I said I fixed a damper actuator and she said she had no idea what that meant but she was glad.

Nudge: "What would you lose if you weren't the one up in that ceiling?"

Response: I don't know. Probably nothing that matters to anyone but me. Corporate guys fix things too, just with more paperwork. Anyway it was a good day, that's all.

Sunday and I should be relaxing but I spent the morning going over the books again. Karen's brother Dave called yesterday to say his company is doing a round of layoffs and asked if I had any work for him. Electrical background, good with his hands, reliable. The honest answer is I could use another set of hands for the summer rush. The problem is that hiring Dave means I'm either growing the business or I'm doing my brother-in-law a favor, and those two things need different levels of commitment.

If I bring him on and things slow down in October, I'm the one who has to let Karen's brother go. If I don't bring him on, I'm the guy who could've helped family and chose the spreadsheet instead. Karen didn't ask me directly but she didn't have to. The way she handed me the phone said enough.

Nudge: "Whose business is it when family's involved?"

Long week. Three installs, two service calls, and the compressor supplier shorted us on the Lennox order so I had to drive to the Burnsville warehouse myself. But everything got done. Everyone got paid. Truck's gassed up for tomorrow. I'm sitting on the deck with a beer and the yard looks good. That's enough for tonight.

The Comfort Systems recruiter called again. This time Karen answered and talked to the guy for ten minutes before handing me the phone. She'd already told him about my experience, how long I've been running the business, all of it. Like she was selling me. I took the phone into the garage and told the recruiter I'd think about it, same as always.

Karen said afterward that she's not trying to run my life, she just wants me to have a backup plan. A backup plan. Like what I built is something that needs a safety net instead of being the safety net. I've kept this thing running through a pandemic, through supply chain problems, through a two-month stretch where our biggest client went bankrupt and didn't pay. I don't need a backup plan. I need people to stop acting like doing it yourself is the risky option when it's the only option I can actually control.

Nudge: "Is she wrong about wanting a net, or is it about who's holding it?"

Response: Both, I think. I don't mind having a net. I mind someone else deciding what the net looks like. If I go corporate, the stability is real but it's theirs. They can pull it anytime. What I have now is smaller and harder but it's mine. That's the part she doesn't hear when she talks about benefits and salary bands.

First full week done at Birch Lane. The kids are sweet. One boy, Mateo, already told me he wants to read "chapter books like his sister." That kind of thing is why I'm here. The building is old and the supply closet is basically empty, but I spent the weekend printing worksheets at the library and laminating them myself. Cost me about forty dollars out of pocket, which I noticed when I checked my account this morning.

I set up a reading corner with pillows I found at a thrift store. It looks decent. The other second-grade teacher, Mrs. Okada, lent me a box of leveled readers she wasn't using. I mapped out the first unit already and I feel like I actually know what I'm doing for Monday. Haven't felt that way in a while. Small thing, but it matters. The commute is long and the parking lot has potholes, but I walked into that classroom and the kids were waiting and it just felt like where I was supposed to be.

Nudge: "Forty dollars on worksheets your first week — what made that feel worth it?"

Response: Because if I don't set this up right from the start, the kids lose momentum and I lose the year. I've seen it happen during student teaching. You can't get that first month back.

My old cooperating teacher from Edina called today to tell me they still have a position open. Full benefits, union contract, thirty kids max per class. She said she put in a word for me and they'd probably take me without a second interview.

I told her I'd think about it and then I sat in the parking lot for twenty minutes. Birch Lane doesn't even have a functioning copier half the time. But the literacy program here is the whole reason I came. I keep thinking about Mateo and the other kids who just started trusting me. I said no. I think I said no. I told her I'd call back Monday but I already know I'm not going to.

Nudge: "You sat there twenty minutes. What were you arguing with yourself about?"

Response: Whether I'm being stupid. Whether six months from now I'll wish I'd just taken the safe thing.

Barely slept. Kept going over that phone call. The Edina salary would have covered my student loans and left room to breathe. I know exactly what I'm giving up and I still can't make myself call her back.

Birch Lane sent around a memo about possible mid-year budget cuts. Nothing confirmed, but the newer hires would be first to go. That's me. Mrs. Okada told me not to worry, that they say this every fall, but she's been here eleven years so of course she's not worried.

Meanwhile my mom keeps forwarding me job postings from the county school district back home. Steady pay, pension, close to family. Every time she sends one I feel my chest tighten. She's not wrong — those jobs are real and they'd solve a lot of problems. But I keep picturing myself in a fluorescent classroom running a curriculum someone else wrote, and something in me just won't do it. I deleted the emails.

Nudge: "Your chest tightened. What does that feel like exactly?"

Response: Like I said, it's just stress. Everybody's stressed in September.

Had a normal day for once. Taught the short vowel unit, kids did okay on the pre-assessment. Ate lunch in the staff room instead of my classroom, which I almost never do. Talked to the PE teacher about the fall festival coming up. She asked if I wanted to run a booth and I said sure without even thinking about it. That felt easy for once.

After school I graded papers and reorganized the reading corner. Mateo finished his first leveled reader and asked if he could take the next one home. I said yes. His mom came to pick him up and thanked me for sending books home. She said he reads to his little brother at night now. That's the kind of thing that doesn't show up on any evaluation form but it's the whole point.

Drove home, made pasta, watched half a movie. Nothing remarkable. Just a day that went the way it was supposed to. Even the drive home felt calm. No knot in my stomach, no replaying conversations. Just a Tuesday.

Long week but I got through it. The reading groups are actually working. I can see the lower group catching up — two kids moved from level B to level D in three weeks. I stayed late Thursday to regroup the library baskets and it took forever, but now every kid can find a book at their level without asking me.

I'm tired but it's the right kind of tired. Like I did something that mattered today and I can feel it in my body. Haven't felt that way at a job before. Even when the copier jammed twice and I had to hand-write a worksheet on the whiteboard instead.

Nudge: "What does the wrong kind of tired feel like?"

Response: Like when I was student teaching in Edina and I'd come home drained but couldn't point to anything I'd actually built. Just following someone else's plan all day.
Fall festival was today. I ran the book swap booth. Kids brought in old books and traded them. Went fine. Cleaned up after and grabbed dinner with Mrs. Okada.

Ran the numbers this weekend. If I'd taken the Edina job I'd have an extra nine hundred a month after taxes. That's rent and then some. But money isn't the only thing that matters, and I'm tired of everyone acting like it should be.

Nudge: "Who's been saying that to you?"

Response: My mom, mostly. And my roommate from college who just got a salaried position downtown. She keeps asking how I'm "managing."

My mom drove up for the weekend and took me grocery shopping, which was nice until she started asking about my health insurance situation. I told her I'm on the school's plan and it's fine, which is technically true, but the deductible is high and I haven't been to the dentist since graduation. She got quiet in that way she does when she's holding something back.

On the drive home she finally said it. She said she worries about me, that I'm making things harder than they need to be, that there are good teaching jobs closer to home with real benefits and a pension. I know she's right about the math. I know the safer path is right there. But every time someone lays it out for me like that, something in me digs in harder. I don't want to build my life around avoiding the worst-case scenario. I want to build it around something I actually chose.

I didn't say that to her. I just said I'd look into it. But I'm not going to.

Nudge: "What did you actually choose when you picked Birch Lane?"

Response: I chose the kids. Isn't that enough?

Budget update came out. Birch Lane is keeping all current staff through the year. So that's settled, at least until spring. I should feel relieved but instead I just feel annoyed that I spent weeks worrying about it. The worry doesn't go away just because the thing you were worried about didn't happen.

My mom texted this morning asking if I'd thought about "what we talked about." I haven't replied yet. I know what the safer move looks like. I can list the reasons on paper. But I keep choosing this instead and I don't think that's going to change anytime soon. Maybe I'll feel differently when the next round of budget memos comes out, but right now I'm staying.

Spent twenty minutes at the pharmacy comparing two brands of the same medication because the cheaper one had packaging I didn't recognize. Walked out with the expensive one. Too tired to explain to myself why that matters but it does.

Nudge: "What was it about the unfamiliar packaging?"

Went to the office, reviewed next quarter's staffing projections, had lunch with Faisal at the usual place. Came home, called my mother, watched half a documentary about shipping routes before falling asleep on the couch.

My cousin Nasser wants me to co-sign on a car loan for him. He's family, and I know he means well, but his track record with money is not great. I told him I'd think about it, which is the polite version of what I actually felt, which was a hard wall going up the second he asked.

Part of me wondered if I'm being too rigid. He's working now, he's trying. But I've spent three years building a buffer between myself and the kind of financial stress my parents dealt with when I was a kid, and the thought of someone else's payments showing up on my credit report makes my chest tight. I know how that sounds.

Nudge: "That wall — protective or isolating?"

Response: Both, probably. But I'd rather be isolated than exposed.

Got asked in a team meeting if I'd volunteer for the new branch setup in Jeddah. It's a promotion track, everyone knows that. I said I'd need to review the terms first, which bought me time. Staying here is not playing small — it's just not gambling what already works.

Nudge: "What would the terms need to say?"

Meal-prepped for the whole week on Saturday so I wouldn't have to think about food during work days. Reorganized the emergency drawer too — replaced the expired first aid supplies, checked the flashlight batteries. Tomorrow feels manageable because today I made it so.

Faisal told me today he thinks I overthink things. He said it casually, like it was nothing, but it stuck. He was talking about how I checked the office supply order three times before submitting it because last quarter they sent the wrong toner and it took two weeks to sort out. He thinks that's excessive. I think that's why we didn't run out of toner.

But then I'm sitting here at night and thinking about whether double-checking an office supply order is really about the toner or about something else. Whether I do it because it's smart or because the idea of something going wrong, even something small, makes me feel like I'm standing on a ledge. And I don't know. I honestly don't. But the toner was correct this time.

Nudge: "Standing on a ledge over toner — your words."

Still thinking about what Faisal said. And it's not even the toner thing specifically, it's that he said it like it was obvious to everyone around me. Like they all see this thing I do and just tolerate it. That stings more than the actual criticism.

I called my sister after work and she said something similar once, years ago, that I hold on too tight to things. She meant relationships at the time. I don't think I hold on too tight. I think I hold on the right amount and other people let go too easily and then act surprised when things fall apart.

Nudge: "What did your sister mean back then?"

Response: She was upset I kept texting her ex to make sure he returned her stuff. She thought I was overstepping. I thought I was being thorough.

You know what, I'm not going to keep circling this. Faisal has lost his wallet twice this year. Twice. And both times I was the one who helped him cancel his cards because he didn't even know which bank to call. So maybe being careful is not the personality flaw everyone wants it to be.

I run a team of fourteen people. If I operated the way other people seem to think is normal, things would fall through constantly. The reason things don't fall through is because someone checks. Someone follows up. That someone is me and I'm tired of acting like that needs defending.

Slow day at work. We're between project cycles so mostly it was inbox cleanup and a couple of one-on-ones with junior staff. Had kabsa for lunch from the place near the office that's been there since before I started this job. The owner knows my order by now.

After work I went to the gym, did the same circuit I always do, and stopped at the grocery store on the way home. They moved the rice to a different aisle which threw me off for a second but otherwise everything was fine. Watched the news, texted my mother goodnight.

Couldn't sleep so I'm writing this. There's a rumor at work that our department might get restructured. Nothing official, just something Ahmed mentioned in passing, but now I can't stop running scenarios. What if they move me to a different team. What if the new manager doesn't operate the way I've set things up. What if they decide operations can be leaner and I'm the cut.

I have six months of expenses saved. I know that. I've checked. But the knowing doesn't quiet the thing in my chest that says six months isn't enough, that nothing is enough, that the floor could always open. I protected that buffer carefully and part of me wonders if I built it because I'm wise or because I've never really believed the ground would hold.

Nudge: "Six months saved but still not enough — where does enough start?"

Response: When I stop flinching at rumors. Haven't figured out how to save my way there yet.

Took the morning to deal with the new shift scheduling. HR wants us to finalize the December roster by Thursday which means I had to pull two supervisors into a room and sort through everyone's leave requests. Nothing dramatic, just the usual back and forth about who covers what. One of the guys asked for the entire last week of December off and I approved it because he hasn't taken time in months. Didn't think much of it at the time.

Lunch was at my desk again. Brought lentil soup from home but it had gone lukewarm by the time I got to it. I keep telling myself I'll start eating in the break room like a normal person but there's always one more email. After lunch I had the monthly safety review call with the Zarqa site, which went fine, nothing flagged. Then spent the rest of the afternoon updating inventory projections.

Drove home the long way because traffic on the highway was backed up near the Seventh Circle. Put on some Fairuz which was nice. Got home, heated up what was left of last night's mansaf, and sat on the balcony for maybe ten minutes before I started going through tomorrow's agenda on my phone.

Nudge: "That ten minutes on the balcony — what pulled you back inside?"

Response: Nothing dramatic. I just remembered I hadn't confirmed the forklift maintenance slot for Thursday. Once I picked up the phone it was over.

Still up, can't really settle. After that last entry I went and made tea and sat back on the balcony, properly this time, no phone. And it was good for a few minutes, the air was cool and I could hear the mosque down the street and I was just sitting there with my tea. But then this thing started where I'm thinking about how I approved Fadi's leave for December which means I'll need to cover his shifts myself or find someone, and suddenly the tea doesn't taste like anything anymore.

It's stupid. I literally just wanted to sit and drink tea. That's all I was doing. And I couldn't let myself have it for more than five minutes before my head started making it into a problem that I wasn't being productive. I don't even know who I'm performing for at eleven at night on my own balcony.

Nudge: "Who taught you that sitting still needs a reason?"

Response: Everybody gets like this. It's not that deep.
Third time opening this today. Just realized I never actually finished the tea. Found it cold on the railing. Going to bed.

Bought knafeh on the way home from the Zarqa site. Ate it in the car in the parking lot before going upstairs, still warm. Didn't check my phone once.

Nudge: "What made you stay in the car for that?"

Everyone at work acts like I don't know how to take a break. My sister called on Friday and said Mama told her I looked tired at dinner, and then today Rami, one of my supervisors, made some comment about how I should use my own leave balance before telling everyone else to use theirs. And I get it, fine, they have a point on paper. But it's not like I'm some robot who doesn't want to rest. I do. I just can't flip a switch and turn off the part of my brain that keeps running through what needs to get done.

I was genuinely tired today, like the kind of tired where you sit down and your whole body feels heavy. And I thought about going home early, it was a quiet afternoon, nothing urgent. But then I started thinking about how that would look, leaving at three when my team is there until five. Even though nobody would care. Even though I tell my own people to go home early when it's slow. I couldn't give myself the same thing I'd give anyone else without it feeling like I was getting away with something.

So I stayed. Answered emails that could have waited. Drove home in the dark. Made rice and ate it standing in the kitchen. I know this is a pattern. I just don't know what I'm supposed to do differently when the guilt is louder than the tiredness.

Nudge: "You'd send Rami home in a heartbeat. What makes your rest different?"

Response: Because if I rest and something goes wrong, it's my fault. If they rest, I'm the one who covered. That's the deal I made with myself somewhere.

My cousin had a gathering and there was good food and music and I was looking forward to it all week. Sat there the whole time thinking about a procurement deadline on Monday. Left early and felt worse than if I hadn't gone at all.

Made pandan chiffon cake this morning. The kitchen smelled good for hours and I sat at the table eating a slice with hot Milo before anyone else was up. It was nice.

Nudge: "What changes when the house fills up?"

Response: Everything becomes about what needs doing next.

Same day, writing again. Wei Lin called this afternoon and asked me to watch the grandkids next Saturday because she has a work event. I said yes obviously. But it means I'll miss the CNY market at Chinatown with Mei and Hwee Leng. We've been planning it for two weeks.

It's fine. The kids love coming over and I already told Wei Lin I would. Mei said they can go without me and bring me back some bak kwa. I told her to get the thick-cut one from Lim Chee Guan.

Nudge: "Two weeks of planning is a lot to set aside."

Response: It is, but what am I going to say — no, find someone else, I want to go shopping? She's my daughter.

Spent the whole day with the grandkids as expected. They were fine, quite well behaved actually. But by 4pm I was so tired I could barely keep my eyes open. I sat down on the sofa for maybe ten minutes while they were watching cartoons and immediately felt guilty, like I should be doing something — cutting fruit, tidying up, checking if they needed water. So I got up.

Later after Wei Lin picked them up I heated some leftover soup and just stood in the kitchen eating it from the pot. Didn't even sit down. I wanted to take a long bath — the kind with the lavender salts my friend gave me for my birthday — but the bathroom still needed cleaning from the kids using it, and by the time I finished scrubbing I didn't feel like a bath anymore. I just showered and went to bed.

I keep thinking about the ten minutes on the sofa and how I couldn't even let myself have that. The children were fine. They were watching TV. Nothing was on fire. But something in me just wouldn't let it be okay to sit there.

Still tired from yesterday. Woke up with my neck stiff and couldn't really move it properly. Husband said I should go see the TCM doctor but I looked at the week ahead and there's grocery shopping tomorrow, Ma's checkup on Wednesday, and I told Mrs Tan I'd help with the community centre lunar new year cleanup on Thursday.

I know a massage would help. I know lying down with a heat pad for an hour would help. But I keep hearing my mother's voice in my head telling me not to be so pampered, that at her age she was carrying buckets of water uphill and still cooking for seven children. So I put Tiger Balm on it and kept going.

Finished the grocery run this morning and treated myself to a bowl of laksa at the hawker centre after. I almost didn't — the queue was long and I had frozen things in the bag. But I thought, five more minutes won't matter, and I stood in line anyway.

The laksa was really good. Extra hum. I sat at one of those metal tables by myself and didn't look at my phone or think about what was next. Just ate. By the time I was done the ice cream in the grocery bag was starting to soften but honestly it was worth it.

Nudge: "What tipped you into staying in that queue?"

Response: I could smell it. That's really all it was. The coconut milk and the chilli — I just thought, when was the last time I had this?

Valentine's Day, not that it matters to us at this age, but husband brought home a box of those nice mooncake-style pastries from Bengawan Solo after work. The tau sar ones I like. He just left them on the counter and didn't make a big deal about it which is his way.

I wanted to sit down and have one with tea and just enjoy it, the way you see people do in those café videos online. But I was in the middle of folding the laundry and then I remembered I hadn't called Ma today and she gets anxious if I don't call by 7, so I called her first, then finished the laundry, and by then I was standing at the counter eating the pastry over the sink because I was also rinsing out the thermos.

And the whole time I was eating it I was thinking about how I should be enjoying this more, which is such a strange thing to think, because the act of thinking it already means you're not. Like the pastry was good. I know it was good. I could taste it. But my mind was already three tasks ahead. I don't know when eating something nice became just another thing I do between other things. I saved the rest of the box for the grandkids.

Nudge: "You saved the box for the grandkids — was that automatic?"

Response: Yes. I didn't even think about it until you asked. Of course you give the nice things to the children. That's just what you do.

Went to Ma's nursing home today, usual Sunday visit. Brought her the dried tangerine peel she asked for and sat with her for two hours. She complained the aircon was too cold, same as always. I adjusted it, brought her an extra blanket, refilled her water thermos.

On the way home I stopped at the provision shop for dish soap and sponges. Cooked dinner. Washed up. Nothing unusual. I don't know why I'm even writing today, there's nothing to say really. Everything got done, nobody needed anything they didn't get. That should be enough.

Mei asked me last week if I wanted to join her for a foot reflexology session — the new place near Bishan that has the herbal soak. I said I'd think about it and I've been thinking about it for five days now. It's $38 for an hour. That's not a lot of money. We can afford it easily.

But there's this thing where I feel like I need to justify it first. Like I need to have done enough to deserve an hour of someone rubbing my feet. Which is ridiculous when I say it out loud. My husband never thinks twice about buying fishing lures that cost more than that. But for me it feels different. It feels like something I have to earn, and I'm never quite sure I have.

Went to the reflexology with Mei after all. It was good. My feet have been aching from all the standing and walking to the market so it actually made a difference. Mei and I talked the whole time about her daughter's wedding planning and it felt normal, like two friends just doing something together.

But on the MRT home I started thinking about the laundry I didn't hang up before leaving, and whether I'd left the rice cooker on, and I realised I'd chosen the useful thing to worry about instead of just sitting with the fact that I'd had a nice afternoon. The rice cooker was off. The laundry was fine. I just couldn't let the afternoon be what it was.

Nudge: "You caught yourself doing it this time."

People at the plant keep asking why I don't apply for the regional director position now that Héctor is retiring. I know exactly why — the travel, the relocating, none of that sits right with me. My team runs well, my commute is twenty minutes, and I sleep fine at night.

Nudge: "Is there any part of you that's curious about it?"

Response: Not really. Maybe five years ago I would have thought about it differently, but I watched Héctor miss his daughter's quinceañera because he was in Monterrey for a plant inspection. That told me everything I needed to know.

Lucía mentioned her sister's husband lost his job at the autoparts factory in León. Just like that — twenty-two years and they called a meeting on a Friday afternoon. I checked our savings account twice after dinner, which is not something I normally do on a weekday.

Nudge: "What were you hoping to see when you checked?"

Spent Saturday morning fixing the back fence with my compadre Tomás. Felt good to work with my hands for once instead of staring at spreadsheets. Lucía brought us agua de jamaica and we sat in the yard afterward just talking about nothing important.

Corporate sent an email Friday about "restructuring opportunities" in the operations departments across all three plants. The language was carefully neutral, all about efficiency and modernization, but I have been doing this long enough to recognize what that kind of memo actually means. Somebody is going to lose headcount.

I spent part of Sunday looking through my employment contract — the termination clauses, the severance calculations, the notice periods. Lucía noticed me reading it at the kitchen table instead of doing our usual budget review and asked if something was wrong. I told her I was just being thorough, which is true but also not the whole picture.

The thing that bothers me is not the restructuring itself. Companies do this. What bothers me is that I had filed my contract in the closet three years ago and forgot exactly where. It took me twenty minutes to find it. That should not have happened. I need a better system for keeping important documents where I can reach them quickly.

Nudge: "You spent a lot of time on the contract. What did you find?"

Response: The severance is decent — four months for my tenure — but decent is not the same as comfortable. We could cover things for ten months total if I combined severance with savings, but that assumes nothing else breaks. The car is twelve years old, the roof needs work eventually. Ten months sounds like a lot until you start listing what eats into it.

My direct report Andrés came in excited about some new automation software he wants to pilot on the production line. He showed me a demo and honestly it looked impressive — would probably cut processing time by fifteen percent. But the implementation would mean shutting down Line 2 for three weeks and retraining half the floor staff. Three weeks of lost output during our busiest quarter.

I told him to write up a proposal and I would review it, but the truth is I already know I am going to push it to next fiscal year. Maybe the quarter after that. I could hear how flat my voice sounded when I said it and I think he noticed too.

Nudge: "What made your voice go flat?"

Response: Because I know I am probably being the bottleneck. Andrés is sharp, the software looked solid, and fifteen percent is not nothing. But shutting down a line for three weeks when corporate is already talking about restructuring — that is the kind of disruption that puts a target on your department. I would rather deliver predictable numbers and keep everyone employed than chase an improvement that could backfire at the worst possible time. I just wish I could explain that to Andrés without sounding like I have given up.

Got the water bill and it was almost double the usual amount. Probably a leak somewhere. Called a plumber but the earliest appointment is next Thursday, so I have been turning the main valve off at night just in case.

Plumber came, turned out it was just a faulty meter reading — no actual leak. Lucía laughed about me turning the water off every night for five days. I told her better cautious than flooded, and she kissed my forehead and said "sí, mi amor, así eres tú."

The restructuring memo turned into a town hall meeting this week. They are consolidating some administrative roles at the Querétaro plant but operations at our site stays as-is for now. My boss Carlos pulled me aside afterward and said my numbers were part of the reason they kept Guadalajara intact. Sixteen years of consistent output apparently counts for something.

I reorganized the filing cabinet this weekend. Employment contracts, insurance policies, property deed, car title — all in labeled folders now, easy to grab. Lucía thinks I am being excessive but she also put her own documents in the system without me asking, so I think she sees the point even if she would not say it directly.

Nudge: "How did it feel hearing Carlos say that?"

Response: Relieved, obviously, but also something I did not expect — vindicated, maybe. I have spent years being the manager who says no to flashy ideas, who keeps the same routines, who does not chase promotions. Sometimes I wonder if people think that means I lack ambition. But steady output is what saved those jobs. Not innovation, not bold moves. Just showing up and delivering the same thing every quarter. It is not exciting but it held.

Lucía's mother called this morning to say she wants to sell the family house in Morelia and move into one of those assisted living places. Lucía was quiet for a long time after hanging up. Her mother has lived in that house for forty years — it is where Lucía grew up, where we had our wedding reception in the backyard.

The practical side of it is complicated. Lucía's sister wants to buy the house but does not have enough for a down payment and asked if we could co-sign a loan. I have never co-signed anything in my life. My father told me when I was nineteen: never put your name on someone else's debt, no matter who they are. But this is Lucía's family, and the look on her face when her sister called tonight was not something I can just calculate my way around.

I told them both I needed a few days to think. Lucía did not argue, which means she understands my hesitation even if she hopes I land somewhere different than where I am leaning.

Nudge: "Where are you leaning?"

Response: Toward no. I know how that sounds, and I know what it might cost with Lucía's family. But co-signing means if her sister cannot pay, we pay. And if we pay, that is the emergency fund, that is the roof repair, that is everything I have built to make sure we never end up like Lucía's cuñado in León. I would rather help them find another way — maybe lend a smaller amount directly, something we could absorb if it disappeared. But putting our name on a bank loan is a different thing entirely.

Made a proper dinner tonight for the first time in weeks. Not reheated soup or bread and cheese at the desk, but an actual meal — roasted some chicken thighs with lemon and potatoes, opened a half-decent bottle of white wine. Sat at the table instead of the sofa. Used a real plate.

It was good. It was genuinely good. The kitchen smelled like something other than coffee for once. And then about halfway through eating I started doing the mental arithmetic — the two hours I spent cooking and shopping could have been two hours in the library, or two hours on the lit review chapter that's already a week behind. I kept eating but the food tasted different after that. Like I was doing something I needed to justify rather than just having dinner.

Nudge: "What started the counting?"

Response: Nothing specific. It just arrives. One minute I'm eating, the next I'm calculating. I think it's worse when the food is actually good — if it were just toast I wouldn't feel like I'd spent something.

Supervisor cancelled our meeting so I had an unexpected free hour. Walked to the Grote Markt and sat on a bench with a stroopwafel and watched people for a while. Pigeons, tourists, an old man feeding ducks. Didn't think about the thesis once. Small thing but I feel better than I have all week.

Nudge: "What kept the thesis out of your head?"

Barely slept last night. Got up at five because I couldn't stop thinking about the methods chapter, worked until my eyes burned, then sat in the department seminar all morning where a twenty-six-year-old presented her near-complete dissertation like it was nothing. She's been at this for three years. I've been at it for four and I'm still restructuring my framework.

After the seminar I was so tired I could feel it in my bones. The kind where you know a nap or even just lying down for twenty minutes would reset everything. My flat is a ten-minute walk. But I didn't go. I sat in the department lounge instead, drank bad coffee, and opened my laptop. Because what would it look like — the oldest person in the programme, leaving at one in the afternoon to go take a nap? Nobody would have said anything. I know that. But I would have known.

There's a version of me that would have just gone home, slept for an hour, come back sharper. That version seems like a much more sensible person. Instead I stared at a paragraph for three hours without changing a word, drove home at six feeling worse than when I started, and ate cereal standing up. I don't know who I think is keeping score but I keep playing as though someone is.

Same day, later. Couldn't sleep again so I put on a record — Bill Evans, the Village Vanguard sessions — and just sat in the dark for a bit. Didn't try to make it mean anything. The music was good. I'm tired. That's all there is to report tonight.

Nudge: "Was that sitting or giving up?"

Response: Does it matter? I was sitting. The music was on. I'm not going to overanalyse listening to a record at midnight.

Took the train to Delft today for an archive visit. The reading room had those tall windows and good light. Spent three hours with primary sources and it was the kind of work that doesn't feel like work. Bought a slice of appelgebak at a cafe by the canal on the way back. Ate it slowly. No guilt arithmetic this time.

Nudge: "What was different about today?"

Response: I think it's because the cake came after the work. The order matters to me more than I'd like to admit. If I'd had the cake first, I would have spent the archive hours thinking about whether I deserved it. This way it felt earned. Which is a depressing way to relate to a piece of cake, when I write it out.

Back from Delft, sitting at home. My neighbour Pieter invited me for a drink earlier — just a beer on his terrace, nothing complicated. And I wanted to go. It sounded genuinely nice. But the whole time I was there I couldn't stop thinking about the fact that I have a supervisor meeting on Monday and the chapter draft is still rough. Pieter was telling me about his boat, the repairs he's doing, and I was nodding but running paragraphs through my head.

He noticed, I think. Asked if everything was alright. I said I was just tired from the trip. Which was true but not the whole truth. The whole truth is that I don't know how to enjoy a beer on a Thursday evening without a running tally in the background telling me what it's costing.

Spent most of Saturday at the university library. Productive enough — reorganised my notes on the Bijlmermeer case study and drafted two pages that might actually survive the next revision. Not exciting but solid work.

Walked home through the park. The leaves are mostly gone now. Passed a man walking three dogs who looked happier than anyone I've seen in the department all month. Made a pasta with anchovies and garlic for dinner, the one I learned from an Italian colleague years ago. Watched half a documentary about post-war Dutch architecture that I'd been meaning to get to. Fell asleep on the sofa before it finished, woke up at two in the morning with a stiff neck.

Normal day. Nothing to report, really. Sometimes that's fine.

Nudge: "When isn't it fine?"

Today was a choice. My old colleague Hendrika, from the Rotterdam days, was in town and invited me for lunch at a place we used to go — Indonesian rijsttafel, the kind of meal that takes two hours and you leave feeling like you've been properly fed for the first time in months. I haven't seen her since I started the PhD. She's good company, sharp, funny, the sort of person who makes you remember you used to be someone other than a stressed-out student.

I didn't go. I told her I had a deadline, which is technically true in the way that there is always a deadline. What actually happened is I sat at my desk, revised three paragraphs, went to a supervision prep meeting that lasted forty minutes, and spent the rest of the afternoon reading a paper I could have read tomorrow or next week or never. The work was fine. It was useful. But it wasn't urgent, and I knew that when I made the choice.

By evening I was sitting with the familiar feeling of having picked the responsible thing and gotten nothing in return except a quiet kind of bleakness. Hendrika sent a photo of the rijsttafel with a message that said "you were missed." I looked at it for a long time. The food looked incredible. I ate a sandwich at my desk.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd gone?"

Response: I would have enjoyed it. That's the honest answer. I would have had a good meal with a good friend and come back to the same work in the same state it was in before. Nothing would have fallen apart. I know that. I just couldn't make myself believe it at the time.

December already. Dark by half four. Cycling home tonight the air had that cold sharpness that makes you feel awake whether you want to or not. Locked my bike, walked up the stairs, and instead of going straight to the laptop I did something I haven't done in weeks — ran a bath. Not a shower, a proper bath. Found some old bath oil under the sink that I don't even remember buying, probably left over from when Anneke was still visiting.

Lay there for maybe forty minutes. The water was too hot at first, then just right. I could hear the wind outside and the neighbours' television through the wall, some game show. My mind wandered but not to work, which surprised me. I thought about the flat I had in Rotterdam, the one with the balcony where I used to grow herbs. Thought about a holiday I took in Portugal years ago where I spent a whole day doing nothing except swimming and reading on a beach. It felt like remembering a different person.

Got out when the water went cold, put on clean clothes, made tea. Sat at the kitchen table and just drank it. The thesis will be there in the morning. Tonight, I think, this was enough. I don't say that often. I'm trying to notice when I do.

Finished the last batch of grading for the semester and walked home the long way through campus. Snow was piling up on the quad and I could see my breath and I just felt good about it, the routine of it. Same walk, same coat, same apartment waiting with the soup I made this morning.

Nudge: "What is it about the same walk that does it for you?"

Response: I think it's that nothing is asking me to figure anything out. The route is automatic. I just get to exist in it without calculating.

My landlord mentioned he might sell the building in the spring. Just casually, at the mailbox, like it was nothing. I said something like "oh interesting" and went inside and sat on my bed for twenty minutes staring at the wall.

Nudge: "That sat with you, huh?"

Still thinking about what Doug said yesterday. I looked up what happens when a landlord sells a rental property in Ontario and the tenant protections are there on paper but I've heard enough stories from people in my program to know that "renoviction" is basically a verb now. My rent is $1,150 which is absurd for Toronto, everyone tells me that, and if I had to find a new place I'd be looking at $1,800 minimum for something comparable. That's an extra $650 a month I literally do not have.

I keep running the numbers. I could technically absorb it if I picked up more TA hours but then my thesis timeline stretches and my funding window is fixed. I mapped it out on a napkin at the kitchen table and none of the scenarios land well. One of my roommates from undergrad said I should "just deal with it when it happens" and I wanted to throw my phone across the room. Easy to say when your parents own a condo downtown.

The worst part is I chose this apartment specifically because it was cheap and close and I knew the landlord and it felt locked in. I turned down a nicer place in Kensington two years ago because this one felt more predictable. And now the thing I picked for being steady might be the thing that falls out from under me.

Got the renewal notice for my car insurance today. It went up $40 a month. I should call around and compare quotes but I've been putting it off because the semester starts next week and I don't have the bandwidth to sit on hold with insurance companies.

Told myself I'd do it over the break and now the break is basically gone. The payment comes out on the 15th so I'll probably just let it auto-renew and deal with it later. It's not a huge amount but it's another thing eroding the margin I built into my budget.

Nudge: "What usually happens when you push stuff like this down the road?"

Response: I mean it's forty bucks. It's not going to ruin me. I'll get to it in February when things calm down.

My advisor floated the idea of submitting our paper to a conference in Chicago in April. Which is great for the CV, sure, but that means registration fees, flights, hotel. He said the department might cover part of it but "might" is doing a lot of work in that sentence. I nodded and said sounds good and now I'm sitting here wondering why I didn't just ask what "part of it" means.

The thing is I know I should go. Everyone says conferences are how you build a network and it would be my first one. But dropping $800-1,200 on a maybe-partially-funded trip when my housing situation is already shaky feels like standing on a wobbly table and deciding to juggle. I told myself I'd look into it tomorrow but I know that means I'll look at the conference website, get stressed about the costs, and close the tab.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to actually ask him about the funding?"

Doug — the landlord — stopped me in the hallway again. He said he's "had some conversations" with a developer. Not a done deal, his words. But the fact that there are conversations at all means the clock is ticking on something I can't control. I asked if he had a timeline and he shrugged and said these things take months. Months is not a timeline. Months is a fog.

I spent the evening going through rental listings on Kijiji and Facebook Marketplace. Everything in my price range is either a room in a shared house with strangers or a studio above a convenience store forty minutes from campus by transit. The places that are actually livable are all $1,700 and up. I made a spreadsheet comparing options but halfway through I just stopped because none of them felt like real options. They felt like damage control.

My mom called and I didn't tell her about any of it. She worries and then she offers money she doesn't really have and then I feel worse. I said school was going fine and we talked about my cousin's wedding. Hung up and sat in the kitchen doing nothing for a while. I keep trying to plan my way through this but there's nothing to plan around yet. It's just this low hum of something coming and I can't pin it down enough to solve it.

Submitted my thesis chapter draft to my advisor today. Two months ahead of the internal deadline. He seemed surprised, said most students don't get chapter two in until March. I don't know, I just can't function when something is hanging over me unfinished so I blocked out time every morning since November and chipped away at it.

Felt good walking out of his office. Like at least this one thing is where it's supposed to be. The housing stuff is still a mess and the conference money is still a question mark but the thesis is the one part of my life right now where I set a plan and the plan actually held.

Nudge: "What does it feel like when a plan actually holds?"

Response: I mean it feels normal. That's kind of the whole point, it's not supposed to be remarkable that things go the way you set them up to go. I just wish more things worked like that.

My friend Priya asked if I wanted to move in with her and her partner when their lease comes up in May. They're looking at a two-bedroom near Bloor and Ossington and they need a third person for the den. Rent would be $1,300, which is more than I pay now but way less than anything I've been finding on my own. And I know Priya, I've known her since first year, she's consistent. She pays bills on time, she's quiet, she doesn't bring chaos into shared spaces.

I spent the evening going over my budget to see if $1,300 works. It's tight but it works if I keep the TA position next semester, which my advisor basically confirmed. I'd lose my parking spot but I barely drive in winter anyway. The neighborhood is farther from campus but it's on the subway line, door to door maybe thirty-five minutes. I wrote out the pros and cons and the cons are all minor inconveniences. The pros are that I'd know where I'm living in June and I could stop refreshing Kijiji at midnight.

I told Priya I'm in. She sent a thumbs up emoji and said she'd send me the lease details this weekend. It's not the same as having my own place, and it's not the cheap locked-in situation I had before. But I can see the shape of it. I can budget around it. And right now that's enough to let me actually sleep.

Nudge: "Is it actually enough, or is it just better than not knowing?"

Ate the last of the good cheese after lunch today, the one from the market on Saturday. Just sat at the table with it and a coffee and some crackers. Then spent the rest of the afternoon annoyed at myself for not going straight back to the loom, as if ten minutes of sitting with cheese somehow cancelled out the six hours I'd already put in.

Finished the second panel for the Kungsbacka commission today. When I stepped back and looked at it hung up on the temporary rail, I actually liked it. Not in the way where you talk yourself into liking something because you spent forty hours on it. I genuinely liked the way the indigo section catches the light from the side window. I stood there for a while just looking.

Then I let myself leave the studio at four instead of pushing through to six. Walked down to the harbor and sat on the bench near the ferry terminal. Bought a cardamom bun from the kiosk. It was still warm. The whole walk home I felt good, and for once I didn't ruin it by calculating whether I'd done enough that day to justify leaving early. I just walked.

My friend Karin came over tonight and we cooked together. She brought wine and we made a ridiculous amount of pasta and talked about nothing important for three hours. I didn't think about the commission once, which surprises me even writing it now.

Nudge: "What was different about tonight?"

Teaching day. The folk school wanted me to stay for the staff meeting after my afternoon class, so I did. It ran an hour longer than it needed to because people kept going in circles about the spring exhibition. I had opinions but didn't push them because I'm only there two days a week and it's not my place to take over other people's planning.

Someone asked what I'm working on in my own studio and I gave a short answer. I don't like talking about commissioned work with the folk school crowd because it always turns into a conversation about whether commercial work counts as real art. It does, for the record. The Kungsbacka library is going to have my panels on their walls for the next twenty years. But I don't feel like defending that every time.

Long day but a strangely satisfying one. Woke up early because the light was doing that thing it does in November here, grey but somehow bright at the same time, and I wanted to see how it would fall across the third panel. So I was in the studio by seven, still in my wool socks, coffee going cold on the shelf behind me because I forgot about it.

Worked through until about one without stopping. Not because I was forcing myself but because the work was pulling me along. The warp tension on the new section was exactly right and the shuttle was moving the way I wanted. Those stretches don't happen every day and when they do you just stay in them.

After I stopped I heated up leftover soup and ate it standing at the kitchen counter, which I know isn't a proper lunch but it was warm and salty and I was hungry enough that it tasted better than it had any right to. Then I lay down on the sofa for twenty minutes. Just lay there with the radio on low. Didn't set a timer, didn't think about what I should be doing instead. I was tired enough that my body made the decision for me and I didn't argue with it. The afternoon I spent doing the boring parts, measuring and marking the next section, but even that felt okay because the morning had been so good.

Writing again because something small happened after I put this down earlier. I was brushing my teeth before bed and noticed I'd left the studio light on, so I went in to switch it off. Ended up standing there in the dark with just the hallway light coming through the door, looking at the panels. They looked different at night. Quieter.

I almost went back to the loom. There was that pull, that voice that says you could get another hour in. But I didn't. I turned the light off and closed the door. It felt like a small right thing, choosing to stop. Going to bed now.

Nudge: "What made stopping feel right tonight?"

Response: Because the day had already been full. I didn't owe it anything else.

Had a meeting with the regional leads this morning and Marcus just talked right over me when I was laying out the Q2 headcount plan. I was mid-sentence — literally had the slide up — and he just started redirecting the conversation toward his own hiring timeline. Nobody stopped him. Janet, who was running the meeting, just went with it and moved on.

I sat there for the rest of the hour barely saying anything. I had the data. I had prepared for this since Thursday. And it just got swallowed up because Marcus is louder and has been in the room longer. The worst part is I could feel myself doing the thing where I just go quiet instead of pushing back, and I hated it. My chest was tight and I kept rehearsing what I should have said.

Afterwards, one of the other leads came up to me and said "good points earlier" which somehow made it worse. Like, you heard me but you didn't back me up when it counted? I don't know what to do with that.

Nudge: "What stopped you from cutting back in?"

Response: Honestly? The thought that if I pushed it and Marcus pushed back harder, I'd look like the one making a scene. There's this calculation I do instantly — will I come out of this looking stronger or weaker — and in that moment the math didn't feel right. Which I realize sounds insane because saying nothing definitely made me look weaker. I think I'm more afraid of losing ground publicly than I am of just being invisible.

Submitted the facilities request for the new storage bay expansion three weeks ago. Need sign-off from Dave in operations finance before anything moves. Followed up twice, got a "should have something for you by end of week" both times. Third week running.

Meanwhile my team is stacking pallets in the overflow lot and we're burning hours on workarounds that wouldn't exist if someone would just approve the spend. I called Dave's office again at four o'clock, left a voicemail. Sat at my desk staring at the phone for twenty minutes afterward like it was going to ring back.

Nudge: "Is it the wait, or that you have to wait?"

Response: The second one. The delay is annoying but it's the dependency that actually gets to me. I've run the numbers, I know the ROI, and none of that matters because it's Dave's signature on the line. My whole team's efficiency is bottlenecked by someone who probably hasn't even opened the attachment.

Karen from procurement shot down my vendor swap proposal in front of the entire ops meeting. I had solid margins data and she just kept saying "that's not how we onboard vendors here." I folded. Just said fine, we'll keep the current contract, because I could tell nobody else was going to back me up and I wasn't going to beg.

Quiet Saturday. Took Logan to his baseball game, they won 6-3. He made a nice catch in the third inning and looked over at me in the dugout immediately. That part was good.

Spent some time after the game reorganizing the garage because the shelf brackets have been loose for weeks and it was bothering me. Put on a podcast about supply chain trends, half listened. Thought about whether I should bring the vendor swap thing back up on Monday or just let it die. Karen's probably already forgotten about it but I haven't.

Did some meal prep for the week. Texted my brother about mom's birthday dinner logistics — he still hasn't confirmed how many people he's bringing, which is exactly the kind of thing that drives me nuts. Just tell me a number so I can book the table.

Nudge: "What would letting the vendor thing go feel like?"

Response: It's not really about feelings, it's about the fact that we're paying twelve percent more than we need to. Anyway, I'll probably just bring it up again differently. Frame it as a cost reduction initiative instead of a vendor change. Same thing, different packaging.

Had my quarterly review with Janet. She flagged my team's throughput numbers — second highest in the region, up four percent from last quarter. She said it straight, no fluff, and I appreciated that. Then she asked if I'd be willing to present the process improvements at the May leadership summit.

I said yes immediately. Didn't even hesitate. There's a version of me from five years ago who would've been nervous about standing in front of that room, but right now I just want them to see what my team has built. We earned this. I drove home with the windows down and felt genuinely good for the first time in a while.

Nudge: "What's different about you now versus five years ago?"

Response: I mean, five years ago I was still an analyst hoping someone would notice my work. Now I have a team, I have numbers, I have a track record. It's not some deep transformation — I just have receipts now.

New guy on the team, Derek, has been here two months. In the standup today he started explaining the pallet tracking system to me — the one I designed three years ago. He was walking me through it like I was the new hire. I let him finish because I didn't want to embarrass him in front of everyone, but it sat with me all morning.

Later, one of my senior leads, Tanya, mentioned Derek had been asking her questions about "how things work around here" instead of coming to me. Not sure if he just doesn't know the hierarchy or if he's intentionally going around me. Either way, it doesn't feel great.

Amna presented the quarterly vendor review in our Monday meeting. She used my slides. Not the template I made for the department, the actual slides I prepared last week for the director call. She changed the title and rearranged two charts and acted like it was something she had been working on all weekend.

I sat there watching her walk through my analysis of the Faisalabad supplier delays. The phrasing was mine. The framing was mine. She even used the same footnote about container shortages that I pulled from the logistics brief. Nobody else in that room would have noticed because nobody else reads the logistics briefs. But I noticed.

I didn't interrupt. I didn't raise it. My boss nodded along and thanked her for the thorough breakdown. Amna didn't look at me once during the whole thing. That told me she knew exactly what she was doing.

Afterwards I went back to my desk and just sat there for ten minutes. I should have said something in the room. Something small, even. "I think I had similar findings in my director prep." But I froze. I keep replaying it and the thing that bothers me most is not that she did it. It's that I let her.

Nudge: "What stopped you in that moment?"

Response: It wasn't the right setting. You can't start calling someone out in front of the whole team, it would look petty. If I bring it up now it's old news. It's fine. I just need to make sure it doesn't happen again.

Submitted the new vendor onboarding proposal last Tuesday. It's been seven days and nothing from the director. No feedback, no questions, not even a "received, will review." I followed up once on Thursday with a polite message, kept it short, said I was available to discuss at his convenience. Read receipt came through within the hour. Still nothing.

The proposal took me three weeks. I mapped the entire onboarding cycle, identified two bottlenecks where we lose four to five days on compliance checks, built a revised timeline that would cut new vendor integration from six weeks to three and a half. This isn't a suggestion doc. This is a full operational redesign with cost projections.

Meanwhile he had time yesterday to sit with Bilal for forty minutes about the office renovation budget. Bilal is the facilities coordinator. The office renovation is new carpeting and a break room microwave. My proposal would save the company roughly twelve lakh per quarter in faster vendor activation. But sure, let's discuss the microwave first.

I can't push harder without looking desperate. I can't escalate because there's nobody to escalate to except him. So I sit and wait and pretend I have other priorities while the most important project on my desk collects dust in someone else's inbox.

Nudge: "What's harder — the waiting, or having no way to speed it up?"

Response: Having no way to speed it up. Waiting I can handle if there's an end date. This is just open-ended silence. I could walk into his office and ask directly, but then I look like I'm hovering. The proposal speaks for itself. He should have responded by now.

Farah pushed back on the dispatch scheduling changes in front of the whole floor today. She said the new system creates too much pressure on the packing team and that I hadn't consulted them before rolling it out. She wasn't wrong about the consultation part, but the way she said it — standing there with her arms crossed, loud enough for the warehouse guys to hear — it felt like she was making a point that had nothing to do with dispatch schedules.

I backed down. Told her we could revisit the timeline and get input from the packing leads. The words came out reasonable but inside I was furious. The old schedule had three missed shipments last month. Three. I fixed that. She's been here longer than me and I think she resents that I'm the one making these calls now. But I said fine, let's revisit. Because fighting in the middle of the floor would have made me look worse, not better.
Long day. Eid shopping with Ammi after work, then came home to finish the weekly reports. My eyes hurt and I still need to review tomorrow's vendor call brief before sleeping.

Bilal got the conference room booking system changed so department heads need to reserve through his assistant now. Used to be we could just block time directly on the calendar. He sent the email to all managers like it was a building-wide policy but I checked with procurement — it's just him adding a layer because someone double-booked the main boardroom last month.

It's a small thing. I know it's a small thing. But it means if I need the room for a client call at short notice, I have to go through his person first. I already sent a reply asking whether department heads with client-facing responsibilities could retain direct access. Kept it professional. He replied with a smiley face and "let's discuss offline." That means no.

I could go to the director about it but that feels like escalating a room booking dispute, which is exactly the kind of thing that makes a young manager look like she's overreacting. Ammi always says pick your fights carefully. She's right. But the small things add up and each one is a little bit of ground lost.

Nudge: "What ground are you worried about losing?"

Response: The ability to move fast without asking permission. That's the whole reason the dispatch overhaul worked — I didn't wait for committees and approvals, I just did it. Every new process that adds a gatekeeper between me and getting things done slows me down. It's not about a room. It's about whether I can operate at my own pace or everyone else's.

Had a client visit today. The buyer from the UK account came for the factory walkthrough and the director introduced me as "one of our operations team." Not operations manager. Not the person who restructured the entire dispatch flow that got them their last three shipments on time. Just "one of our operations team."

I smiled and shook hands and walked them through the floor like nothing happened. The buyer asked good questions about our quality checks and I answered every single one without hesitating. By the end she was directing her questions to me, not the director. That felt like something. But the introduction stung. I'm not "one of" anything. I run that floor.

Nudge: "She ended up directing questions to you — does that make the introduction matter less?"

Still thinking about yesterday. Mentioned it to Abba over dinner and he said I should be grateful to have the position at all at my age, that most girls from our family are still finishing their degrees or married by now. He didn't mean it badly, I know that. He's proud of me in his way. But "grateful" is not the word I want to hear when someone erased my title in front of a client.

I wanted him to be angry for me. Instead he told me the story about how his boss at the textile mill passed him over for promotion three times before finally giving it to him. Like patience is the lesson. Like waiting is the same thing as earning. I've already earned this. I just need the people around me to see it the way I do.
Vendor call went smoothly, secured better terms on the denim lot. Farah actually came to me afterwards and said the new scheduling format is working. Small acknowledgment but I'll take it.

Director finally responded to the onboarding proposal. Three weeks of silence and then a five-line email: "Good effort. Let's pilot with one vendor. Loop in procurement." No mention of the cost projections. No acknowledgment that I spent three weeks mapping the entire cycle. "Good effort" — like I'm an intern who turned in a decent assignment.

I should be happy. He approved it, technically. But "loop in procurement" means I lose sole ownership. Now it's a cross-functional initiative, which sounds collaborative on paper but really means I'll be coordinating with people who didn't build this and don't understand the logic behind the sequencing. I built something precise and now it gets diluted. I keep telling myself a pilot is better than no response. But I wanted him to see the full scope and say, "This is exactly what we needed."

Nudge: "What would 'enough' recognition look like for you?"

Cousin's engagement party last night. Every aunty asked what I do and when I said manager they looked confused, then asked about marriage plans. One of them said to Ammi, "At least she's keeping busy."

Nudge: "When they said that, who did you want to correct — them, or yourself?"

Response: Them. Obviously them. I don't need correcting. But you can't lecture your khala at a mehendi about what operations management means. So I smiled and ate my biryani and talked to my cousin about her lehenga instead. Some rooms aren't worth the fight.

Celine asked me again today if I'd reconsidered the flatshare. She found a third person, a ceramicist she knows from art school, and said it would bring my rent down by almost three hundred euros a month. I told her I'd think about it, which is what I always say. The truth is I already ran the numbers. Even with the savings, the lease is month-to-month, the building doesn't have proper insurance, and I have no idea if these two people pay on time.

I know she thinks I'm making excuses. She said something like, "You could use that money for supplies," which is true, but supplies don't matter if I can't pay rent in December because someone flaked. I stayed quiet after that and she changed the subject. I keep having this feeling like I'm making the boring choice and everyone can see it, but I also know I'd lie awake every night in that flat wondering when the arrangement would fall apart.

Nudge: "What would that place need to have for you to say yes?"

Response: A proper lease. All three names on it, with a clause about what happens if someone leaves. Basically the same thing I have now but cheaper. Which I know is the whole reason they don't want to do it that way. So there's my answer, I guess.

Frame shop was quiet today. Restrung two canvases, swept up, closed early because Monsieur Girard had a dentist appointment. Walked home along the Rhone and picked up bread on the way. Nothing to report.

Got the confirmation email for the autumn group show at Galerie Moreau. Three pieces, one wall, six weeks. I actually teared up a little, which is embarrassing to write. But I've been working toward this since January and it's the first time someone picked my work without me having to explain it first.

Spent the morning at the bank because the online transfer for the savings account wasn't going through. Turns out there's a new verification step they added and I needed to update my phone number in person. So that was an hour and a half of my day gone, sitting in a plastic chair watching a number on a screen count up. But I got it sorted. The auto-transfer is set for the 2nd of every month again and the buffer is back where it should be.

The part that's sitting with me is what the woman at the counter said. She asked what the savings were for and I said it was just a general reserve. She sort of smiled and said, "Most people your age don't bother." She meant it as a compliment, I think. But it made me feel strange, like I'm doing something unusual by not wanting to end up stuck. Like it's weird to have a plan for what happens if the freelance work dries up or if Girard closes the shop.

I don't think it's weird. My parents went through a period when I was fifteen where money was genuinely scary, and I remember what that did to my mother's face for about two years. I just don't want to be in that position. But hearing someone point out that most people my age don't do this — I don't know. It made the whole thing feel heavier than it needs to be.

Nudge: "What was it about your mother's face specifically?"

Response: I don't know, it's not one thing. She just looked tired all the time in a way that went deeper than tired. Like she was doing calculations in her head constantly. Anyway, the point is the transfer is fixed now. I'll check it next month to make sure it actually went through automatically.

Back from the bank thing this morning, then spent the afternoon finishing the second piece for the Moreau show. Ink work on the hands came out wrong twice and I had to start the panel over. Eyes are burning. Going to eat leftovers and sleep.

Had a really good afternoon at the shop. A woman came in to get an old watercolour framed — her grandmother's, she said. We spent maybe twenty minutes going through mat options and she kept asking my opinion like it actually mattered. I ended up suggesting a linen mount with a thin oak frame, which is what I would have done for my own work. She seemed genuinely happy. Girard nodded at me from the back, which from him is basically a standing ovation.

I think the reason the frame shop works for me, beyond the money, is that it's predictable. I know what Tuesdays look like. I know what Girard expects. There's a rhythm to it that makes the freelance days feel less chaotic by comparison. Some of my friends think I should quit and go full-time on illustration, and maybe they're right eventually, but right now having those three shifts is like having a floor under me. Everything else can wobble as long as the floor holds.

Nudge: "Is the floor something you want to keep, or something you want to stop needing?"

Had dinner with Celine and Hugo last night. Hugo just quit his job at the design agency to freelance full-time. No savings, no backup plan, just "I'll figure it out." Everyone at the table was congratulating him like he'd done something brave. Celine looked at me at one point and said, "See? It's possible." As if I don't know it's possible. That's not the question.

The question is what happens when it doesn't work out and there's nothing underneath you. But I didn't say that, because I'd sound like someone's aunt. I just smiled and said good for him. I meant it, mostly. But the whole walk home I was annoyed, not at Hugo specifically, just at this idea that keeping something steady is the same as not trying. I am trying. I'm showing at Moreau in October. I just also happen to have rent covered through March regardless of whether anyone buys anything. That's not fear, that's just not being stupid.

Wednesday. Frame shop in the morning, then home to work on the third Moreau piece. This one's coming together faster than the other two — the composition sorted itself out in my sketchbook last week and I've mostly been executing. Used the good ink I've been rationing since April. Felt right to use it now rather than save it for something hypothetical.

After dinner I updated my invoice spreadsheet. Two freelance payments came in this week — the book cover for that small press in Marseille, and the editorial spot for the food magazine. Both on time, which doesn't always happen. Logged them, moved the savings portion over, updated the running total. It's not a lot but it's growing. At this rate I'll hit my year-end target by November.

I also renewed my phone contract today instead of switching to the cheaper provider Celine recommended. Her provider has better rates but they lock you into a twelve-month plan with an exit fee, and I've heard mixed things about their coverage outside the city. Mine costs eight euros more per month but I've had it for four years and it just works. Sometimes I think the small extra cost is worth not having to think about it.

Nudge: "You're paying for certainty a lot — the lease, the phone, the frame shop. Is that ever too much?"

Response: Maybe. I add it up sometimes and think, if I dropped all the safe choices I'd have an extra four or five hundred euros a month. That's materials, that's a short course, that's two months closer to not needing the frame shop at all. But then I imagine actually doing it and my chest gets tight. It's like — I know the maths says take the risk, but my body won't let me. I don't know if that's wisdom or just a thing I haven't worked through yet.

Girard mentioned today he might cut hours in September because business slows down after the tourists leave. Just a possibility, he said. But I've already started thinking about what I'd do if he cuts my shifts to two, or one, or none.
